title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text: 📝 Note to self: These notes are a summary of the live lecture transcript. Review alongside the slides posted on campus and the lab exercises. Paula mentioned she will upload exercises after the break — revisit and annotate those sections once available.

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.1 Multiple Linear Regression
Multiple linear regression is an extension of simple linear regression. Instead of one explanatory (independent) variable \(X\), you now have multiple explanatory variables \(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_k\).
The model looks like this:
\[
Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_k X_k + \varepsilon
\]
The word “linear” here does not mean the relationship between \(X\) and \(Y\) is a straight line — it means that the response variable \(Y\) is a linear function of the coefficients (\(\beta\) values). This is an important distinction.

2.1.1 Interpreting the Coefficients
Each coefficient \(\beta_j\) represents the expected change in the response \(Y\) for a one-unit increase in \(X_j\), while all other explanatory variables are held constant.

⚠️ Exam tip (Paula’s emphasis): When interpreting a coefficient in a multiple regression model, you must state that the other variables are held constant. This is key to getting full marks.

Example from class (savings dataset):

For a one-unit increase in population under 15, the savings rate decreases by 0.216, holding all other variables constant.
For a one-unit increase in percentage rate of change of disposable income (DDPI), the savings rate increases by 0.443, holding all other variables constant.

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.2 Matrix Notation
The entire regression model can be written compactly using matrix notation:
\[
\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}
\]
Where:








Symbol
Meaning
Dimensions




\(\mathbf{Y}\)
Vector of response values
\(n \times 1\)


\(\mathbf{X}\)
Design matrix (includes a column of 1s for the intercept)
\(n \times (k+1)\)


\(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)
Vector of coefficients (intercept + slopes)
\((k+1) \times 1\)


\(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\)
Vector of error terms
\(n \times 1\)



The first column of \(\mathbf{X}\) is a column of 1s — this is a placeholder for the intercept \(\beta_0\).

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.3 Least Squares Estimation
The least squares method finds the \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) that minimises the error sum of squares (SSE) — i.e., it minimises the total squared distance between the observed values and the fitted (predicted) values.
\[
SSE = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}})^T(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}})
\]
The solution (in matrix form) is:
\[
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{Y}
\]
The fitted values are then:
\[
\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}
\]
Where \(\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\) is known as the Hat Matrix (because it “puts the hat” on \(Y\)).
The residuals are:
\[
\hat{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}} = \mathbf{Y} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}
\]
The estimate of the error variance (\(\sigma^2\)) is:
\[
\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{SSE}{n - k - 1}
\]
Where \(n\) = number of observations and \(k\) = number of explanatory variables.

📌 Paula’s note: You don’t need to compute this by hand — just understand the theory behind it.

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.4 Assumptions of Multiple Linear Regression
The assumptions are the same as in simple linear regression:

Linearity — \(Y\) is a linear function of the coefficients.
Independence — Error terms are independent of each other.
Normality — Errors are normally distributed: \(\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)\).
Constant variance (homoscedasticity) — The variance of the errors is constant across all values of \(X\).
Explanatory variables are measured without error — No distributional assumptions are placed on the \(X\) variables themselves.

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.5 R² and Adjusted R²
R² (R-squared / Coefficient of Determination): The proportion of variation in \(Y\) that is explained by the model. Always expressed as a percentage.
\[
R^2 = 1 - \frac{SSE}{SST}
\]

\(R^2 = 0.2075\) means 20.75% of the variation in Y is explained by X.
Whatever is not explained is due to other unknown variables or random error.

Problem with R²: It always increases (or stays the same) when you add more variables to the model, even if those variables are useless. This can make a model look better than it is.
Adjusted R² penalises the model for adding extra variables that don’t genuinely improve fit:
\[
\bar{R}^2 = 1 - \frac{SSE/(n-k-1)}{SST/(n-1)}
\]

⚠️ The Adjusted R² will always be lower than R² in a multiple regression model. This is expected and not a problem.


📌 Also note: R² for the model with both explanatory variables (28.75%) is less than the sum of the two individual model R² values (20.75% + 9.29% = 30.04%). This is because the two variables share some overlapping explanatory power.

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.6 The Global F-Test
Before looking at individual coefficients, the first step when interpreting a regression output is to check the overall model significance using the global F-test.
Hypotheses: \[H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \cdots = \beta_k = 0\] \[H_1: \text{At least one } \beta_j \neq 0\]

If the p-value is significant → at least one explanatory variable is associated with the response.
This test is called the global F-test (also: comparing the full model to the null model).


⚠️ Exam tip: Always check the overall model p-value first, before interpreting individual coefficients.

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.7 Comparing Nested Models
Sometimes you want to compare a full model (all variables) with a reduced model (fewer variables).
This is done using an ANOVA F-test:
\[
F = \frac{(SSE_{\text{reduced}} - SSE_{\text{full}}) / (p - q)}{SSE_{\text{full}} / (n - p)}
\]
Where: - \(p\) = number of parameters in the full model - \(q\) = number of parameters in the reduced model - \(n\) = number of observations

⚠️ Important: The summary output in R tests only one variable at a time. If you want to drop more than one variable, you must use ANOVA to compare models directly.

Example from class:
Testing whether population over 75 and DPI could both be dropped: F-statistic = 1.723, p-value = 0.19 → fail to reject H₀ → the larger model does not fit significantly better, so those two variables can potentially be dropped.

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.8 Collinearity (Multicollinearity)
Collinearity occurs when two or more explanatory variables are highly correlated with each other.
In the savings dataset example, population under 15 and population over 75 both measure age-related demographics and were negatively correlated with each other. Including both made one or both appear insignificant — even though each was significant on its own.
Effects of collinearity: - Imprecise (unstable) estimates of \(\hat{\beta}\) with large standard errors - F-tests and t-tests may fail to reveal significance - The sign or magnitude of a coefficient can flip when variables are added or removed
Detection — Variance Inflation Factor (VIF):
\[
VIF_j = \frac{1}{1 - R^2_j}
\]
where \(R^2_j\) is the R² from regressing \(X_j\) on all other explanatory variables.

📌 Rule of thumb: If VIF > 5, there is evidence of serious collinearity.

Solutions: - Remove one of the correlated variables - Use Principal Component Analysis (PCA) followed by regression — this creates a new, uncorrelated set of variables - Use shrinkage methods (e.g., Ridge regression, LASSO)

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.9 Model Diagnostics
After fitting a regression model, you must check the assumptions using diagnostic plots. Paula emphasised that these will appear in the labs.

2.9.1 1. Residuals vs Fitted Values

Plots the residuals (\(y_i - \hat{y}_i\)) on the y-axis against the fitted values (\(\hat{y}_i\)) on the x-axis.
What you want: A random, constant scatter of points with no pattern.
What’s bad: A curved or funnel-shaped pattern — this suggests the relationship is non-linear or the variance is non-constant (heteroscedasticity).


💡 If a curved pattern appears, you may need to add a quadratic term (e.g., \(X^2\)) to the model.



2.9.2 2. QQ Plot (Quantile-Quantile Plot)

Compares the quantiles of your residuals to the quantiles of a theoretical normal distribution.
What you want: Residuals that fall close to the diagonal line.
What’s bad: Strong deviations from the diagonal suggest non-normality.


📌 QQ plots are preferred over histograms (especially for small samples), because histograms are sensitive to bin width choice. You can use both together.



2.9.3 3. Leverage
Leverage measures how much an observation’s value on the explanatory variable(s) differs from the mean of those variables.
\[
h_{ii} = \text{diagonal entry of Hat Matrix } \mathbf{H}
\]

An observation with \(X\) equal to the mean has zero leverage.
High leverage → the observation is far from the mean of \(X\) → it has the potential to influence the regression slope greatly.


📌 Rule of thumb: Flag observations where leverage \(> 2(k+1)/n\).



2.9.4 4. Studentised Residuals
Standardised version of the residuals used to identify outliers.

📌 Rule of thumb: An observation may be an outlier if \(|r_i| > 2\).


Note: Standardised and studentised residuals are sometimes defined differently — double-check how R computes them.



2.9.5 5. Cook’s Distance
Cook’s Distance measures the overall influence of a single observation on all the fitted values. It is proportional to the sum of squared differences between predictions made with and without that observation.

If the predictions are the same with or without the observation → no influence.
If the predictions differ → influential observation.


📌 Rule of thumb: Flag observations with Cook’s Distance \(> 1\).



2.9.6 Combined interpretation



Standardised Residuals
Leverage
Influence




Low
Low
Small


High
High
Highly influential

title: Week 9 Study Guide: Multiple Linear Regression
text:
2.10 Cross-Validation
A model may fit the sample data well but fail to generalise to the broader population. Cross-validation tests this.
K-fold cross-validation process:

Randomly partition the dataset into \(K\) equal-sized subsamples.
Fit the model on \(K - 1\) of the subsamples.
Validate (test) the model on the one subsample left out.
Repeat \(K\) times so every subsample is used as a validation set exactly once.
Average the performance metrics across all \(K\) runs to get a single estimate.


📌 Bootstrapping is another method for assessing generalisation — Paula mentioned it briefly.

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
1.1 Research Questions and Hypotheses
Before any data analysis can begin, you must start with a clear research question. This is the foundation of your entire study.

1.1.1 What is a Good Research Question?
A good research question has three essential characteristics:
1. Simple - Contains one predictor variable and one outcome variable - More complex hypotheses with multiple predictors or outcomes should be broken into multiple simple hypotheses - For example, instead of studying “different types of smoking,” simplify to “smoking vs non-smoking”
2. Specific - Leaves no ambiguity about the subjects or variables being studied - Clearly defines how statistical significance will be tested - Uses precise operational definitions that summarize: - The nature and source of the subjects - How variables will be measured - Whether variables are continuous or categorical
3. Stated in Advance - The hypothesis must be determined before examining the data - Prevents the temptation to test multiple hypotheses after seeing results - Minimizes the opportunity to “fish” for significant findings


1.1.2 Why Being Specific Matters
When your hypothesis is specific, you: - Communicate the study clearly to others - Minimize the chance of testing something different once you see the data - Avoid substituting different measurements of variables after the fact - Establish clear variable types (continuous vs categorical) upfront

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
1.2 Study Planning

1.2.1 Research Plan Components
Your research plan should document:

Study Design: How will data be collected? (observational vs experimental)
Target Population: Who or what will you study?
Sample Size: How many subjects will you need?
Predictor and Outcome Variables: What are you measuring and why?



1.2.2 Conceptualizing the Target Population
Before collecting data: - Define the specific population you’re interested in - Establish inclusion criteria: What characteristics define who belongs in your study? - Establish exclusion criteria: Who should NOT be included?
For example, if studying heart disease in adults, you might include: - Inclusion: Adults aged 40-65 with no previous heart conditions - Exclusion: Pregnant women, individuals with genetic heart disease


1.2.3 Sampling Strategy
Once you define your population, decide how to select subjects:
Probability Sampling (preferred for statistical inference) - Every member of the population has an equal chance of being selected - Allows you to generalize findings to the larger population
Non-Probability Sampling - Subjects are selected based on convenience or specific criteria - Makes it harder to generalize results

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
1.3 Statistical Design Concepts
When designing your study, understand these key concepts:







Concept
Definition




Response Variable
The outcome you’re measuring (what you want to explain)


Factor
An independent variable in your study


Level
The different values or categories of a factor


Treatment
The specific condition applied to subjects


Replication
Using multiple subjects per treatment group


Randomization
Randomly assigning subjects to treatment groups


Blocking
Grouping similar subjects to reduce variation


Factorial Design
Studying multiple factors simultaneously


Main Effect
The effect of one factor on the response


Interaction Effect
How one factor’s effect depends on another factor

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
2.1 Measurement Scales

2.1.1 Why Measurement Matters
The type of measurement scale you use affects: - How precise your data is - What statistical tests you can perform - The statistical power of your analysis - Your ability to answer the research question


2.1.2 Continuous vs Categorical Variables
Continuous Variables (Preferred) - Can take any value within a range - Contain more information than categorical variables - Improve statistical power (ability to detect true effects) - Allow for smaller sample sizes - Examples: Age (years), Income (dollars), Blood pressure (mmHg)
Categorical Variables - Fall into distinct categories - Contain less information - Require larger sample sizes for same power - Examples: Smoking status (yes/no), Gender (M/F), Education level (HS/College/Grad)
Key Principle: Always collect data as continuous if possible, even if you might later categorize it. This preserves information and gives you more analytical flexibility.

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
2.2 Measurement Quality: Precision and Accuracy
These two concepts are not the same and are equally important for valid research.

2.2.1 Precision
Definition: How consistently a measurement produces the same value when repeated.

Reflects reproducibility, reliability, and consistency
Affected by random error (chance variation)
Determines the statistical power of your study
Greater precision = greater power to detect effects at the same sample size

Sources of Random Error (Reduces Precision):

Observer Variability: Differences in how observers take measurements

Different observers have different skill levels
Same observer may measure differently on different occasions

Instrument Variability: Measurement tools are not perfect

Environmental factors (temperature, humidity)
Mechanical drift or wear in instruments
Calibration issues

Subject Variability: Natural variation within subjects

Biological fluctuations (heart rate changes throughout the day)
Behavioral variation (mood, fatigue)




2.2.2 Accuracy
Definition: The degree to which a measurement actually represents what it’s supposed to measure.

Reflects the validity of the measurement
Determined by systematic error (consistent bias)
Critical for the validity and generalizability of your findings
Accuracy and precision are not necessarily linked

Important: You can have: - ✓ High precision AND high accuracy (ideal) - ✗ High precision but low accuracy (measurements are consistent but wrong) - ✗ Low precision but high accuracy (scattered measurements but correct on average)


2.2.3 The Relationship Between Precision and Accuracy
Think of archery or darts:

Accurate but imprecise: All shots hit the target area on average, but they’re spread out
Precise but inaccurate: All shots cluster together, but far from the bullseye
Both: All shots cluster in the bullseye

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
3.1 What is EDA?
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) is the critical first step after data collection. You examine your data to:

Summarize the main characteristics
Identify patterns, trends, and relationships
Discover anomalies and outliers
Visualize the distribution of variables
Check for missing data
Assess whether data violates statistical assumptions


3.1.1 The Anscombe’s Quartet Lesson
Why Visualization Matters
Anscombe’s quartet demonstrates why you must visualize your data before analyzing it.
This dataset consists of four different datasets that have: - Identical means (9.00 for x variables; 7.50 for y variables) - Identical standard deviations (3.16 for x; 1.94 for y) - Identical correlation coefficients (r = 0.82 for all pairs) - Identical regression lines
However, when plotted as scatterplots, the four datasets look completely different:

A perfect linear relationship
A curved (non-linear) relationship
A perfect linear relationship with one outlier
No relationship in the data, but a single extreme point drives the correlation

The Lesson: Summary statistics alone can be deeply misleading. Always create graphs to visualize your data.

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
3.2 Components of Initial Data Analysis

3.2.1 1. Summarizing and Visualizing Data
Numerical Summaries - Mean, median, standard deviation - Percentiles and quartiles - Min and max values
Visual Methods - Histograms (show distribution shape) - Box plots (show median, quartiles, outliers) - Scatter plots (show relationships) - Line plots (show trends over time)


3.2.2 2. Univariate Profiling
Examining each variable individually: - What is the distribution shape? - Are there outliers? - Is the variable skewed? - What is the range of values?


3.2.3 3. Bivariate Profiling
Examining relationships between pairs of variables: - Scatter plots for continuous variables - Cross-tabulations for categorical variables - Correlations between continuous variables


3.2.4 4. Data Quality Checks

Missing data extent and patterns
Outliers and unusual values
Data entry errors
Inconsistencies in the data

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
4.1 Understanding Missing Data
Definition: Missing data occurs when values are not recorded for some variables in some observations.

4.1.1 Why Data Goes Missing
Missing data can result from: - Experimental or procedural errors - Data entry mistakes - Survey respondents skipping questions - Equipment malfunction - Subject dropout (in longitudinal studies)


4.1.2 Why Missing Data Is a Problem

Reduces sample size - Fewer observations means less power
Can introduce bias - If missingness is related to the values
Complicates analysis - Many statistical tests cannot handle missing values
Violates assumptions - Listwise deletion can change the nature of your sample

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
4.2 Identifying Missing Data Patterns

4.2.1 Types of Missing Data
Ignorable Missing Data - Expected and part of the research design - No action is necessary - Example: Surveys where certain questions only apply to some respondents (e.g., “Describe your pregnancy” is ignorable for non-pregnant respondents)
Non-Ignorable Missing Data - Results from either a known or unknown missing data process - Due to procedural or experimental factors - Requires investigation and action


4.2.2 Three Mechanisms of Missingness
Understanding why data is missing is crucial for choosing the right solution:
1. Missing Completely at Random (MCAR) - The probability of missingness is the same for all units - Causes of missing data are unrelated to the data itself - Example: A survey respondent randomly decides whether to answer each question by flipping a coin - Implication: Simple deletion of missing values is acceptable (though it reduces power)
2. Missing at Random (MAR) - Missingness depends on observed variables, not the missing value itself - The probability of missingness varies by group but is predictable from other data - Example: Older respondents are more likely to skip the “earnings” question, but this depends only on age (which you observe) - Implication: Imputation methods that use other variables can produce valid estimates
3. Not Missing at Random (MNAR) - Missingness depends on the missing value itself - The probability of being missing is related to what the value would have been - Example: People with higher incomes are less likely to report their earnings - Implication: This is the most problematic situation; bias is hard to correct

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
4.3 Steps to Address Missing Data

4.3.1 Step 1: Determine the Type
Is the missing data ignorable (part of study design) or not ignorable (unexpected)?


4.3.2 Step 2: Determine the Extent

How much data is missing for each variable?
How many complete cases are there?
Is the amount small enough to not affect results?



4.3.3 Step 3: Diagnose the Randomness
Assess whether missingness is MCAR, MAR, or MNAR by examining: - Patterns across variables - Relationships with other observed variables - Domain knowledge about why data might be missing


4.3.4 Step 4: Select an Imputation Method
If Ignorable or Minimal Missing Data - Deletion: Remove observations with missing values (acceptable if MCAR)
If More Substantial Missing Data

Mean Imputation: Replace missing values with the variable mean

Advantage: Simple
Disadvantage: Distorts the distribution, underestimates variance

Regression Imputation: Use regression to predict missing values

Advantage: Uses relationships with other variables
Disadvantage: Creates artificially perfect predictions

Stochastic Regression Imputation: Regression + random noise

Advantage: Preserves variability
Better than deterministic imputation

Prediction Methods: Use k-Nearest Neighbors or other algorithms

Find similar cases and use their values

Last Observation Carried Forward (LOCF): For longitudinal data

Use previous measurement value
Conservative (tends to underestimate change)

Multiple Imputation: Create multiple complete datasets

Most sophisticated approach
Accounts for uncertainty in imputed values

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
4.4 Practical Implementation

4.4.1 In R
# Check for missing values
is.na(x)                    # TRUE if x is missing
sum(is.na(x))              # Count missing values

# Remove missing data
mean(x, na.rm=TRUE)        # Calculate mean, ignoring NAs
complete.cases(df)         # Identify complete rows
na.omit(df)               # Remove rows with ANY missing values
df <- df[complete.cases(df), ]  # Keep only complete cases


4.4.2 Decision Tree

Is data ignorable? → NO ACTION needed
How much is missing?

< 5% per variable → Simple deletion acceptable
5-30% per variable → Consider imputation
> 30% per variable → Consider removing variable entirely

Is it MCAR, MAR, or MNAR?

MCAR → Deletion or simple imputation acceptable
MAR → Use regression or predictive imputation
MNAR → Advanced methods, or acknowledge bias

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
5.1 What Are Outliers?
Definition: Outliers are observations with unusual combinations of characteristics that make them distinctly different from other observations.
An outlier typically has: - An unusually high or low value on a single variable, OR - A unique combination of values across multiple variables

5.1.1 Example
In a dataset of student test scores: - A score of 5/100 is an outlier (very low) - A student with high math AND low reading scores might be an outlier (unusual combination)

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
5.2 Classifying Outliers

5.2.1 Sources of Outliers
1. Procedural Error - Cause: Measurement error, data entry mistake, equipment malfunction - Action: Investigate and correct if possible; delete if unfixable - Example: A blood pressure reading recorded as “2000 mmHg” (clearly impossible)
2. Extraordinary Event - Cause: Real but exceptional circumstance during data collection - Action: Decide whether to keep based on research context - Example: A patient’s extremely low blood pressure during a severe medical crisis
3. Extraordinary Observation - Cause: The subject is genuinely unusual or represents a rare population segment - Action: May keep but note; requires domain expertise - Example: An exceptionally tall person in a height study (biologically valid but extreme)

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
5.3 Handling Outliers
Important: Outliers should be considered within the context of your analysis.

5.3.1 Before Taking Action

Verify the value - Is it a real measurement or an error?
Understand the cause - Why is this observation unusual?
Check implications - Does removing it change your conclusions significantly?
Consider domain knowledge - Is this observation valid in your field?



5.3.2 Options

Keep: If it represents a real, valid observation
Investigate & Correct: If it’s a measurement or data entry error
Remove: If it’s a true error with no way to verify
Analyze With and Without: Report both analyses to show sensitivity

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
6.1 Why Assumptions Matter
Before fitting a statistical model, you must verify that your data satisfies the model’s assumptions. Different models have different requirements.

6.1.1 Key Assumptions for Linear Models
1. Linearity - The relationship between predictor (x) and outcome (y) is linear - Check: Scatter plot should show a linear pattern - Violation: Use transformation or non-linear models
2. Independence - Observations are independent of each other - Violations commonly occur in: time series, repeated measures, clustered data - Check: Study design (randomization, no confounding)
3. Homoscedasticity (Constant Variance) - The variance of errors is constant across all levels of the predictor - Check: Scatter plot around regression line should have consistent spread - Violation: Variance increases/decreases with predictor values
4. Normality - The errors (residuals) are normally distributed - Check: Q-Q plot (quantile-quantile plot) or histogram of residuals - Note: Large sample sizes make violation less problematic due to Central Limit Theorem - Violation: Use transformation, non-parametric tests, or larger sample

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
6.2 Data Transformation

6.2.1 When to Transform
If your data violates assumptions, transformation can sometimes help by: - Stabilizing variance - Making distributions more normal - Making relationships more linear


6.2.2 Common Transformations
For Skewed Data



Transformation
Use When
Effect




Square root
Mild skewness
Gentle transformation


Log
Moderate skewness, positive data
Strong transformation


Reciprocal (1/x)
Severe skewness
Very strong transformation


Box-Cox
Optimal transformation unknown
Data-driven selection



For Other Issues

Standardizing: Convert to mean=0, SD=1
Squaring: If relationship is non-linear
Combinations: First transform, then analyze



6.2.3 Important Considerations

Interpretation changes: After transformation, you interpret results on transformed scale
Assumptions still apply: Transformation must result in assumptions being met
Not always possible: Some violations cannot be fixed by transformation alone
Alternative approaches: Non-parametric tests or robust methods may be better

title: Week 1: Data Analysis Protocol
text:
7.1 Key Takeaways

Define clearly: Your research question must be simple, specific, and stated in advance
Plan carefully: Study design includes population definition, sampling, measurement, and sample size
Measure well: Collect data as continuous when possible; ensure both precision (consistency) and accuracy (validity)
Explore first: Always visualize and summarize before modeling (Anscombe’s quartet lesson!)
Handle missing data: Identify type and mechanism; choose appropriate method (deletion vs imputation)
Check assumptions: Verify linearity, independence, homoscedasticity, and normality before modeling
Transform if needed: Use transformations (log, square root) to fix violations
Use domain expertise: All decisions about outliers, missing data, and transformations should be informed by subject matter knowledge

title: Week 4
text:
2.1 What is Experimental Design?
Experimental design is the process of planning a study so that you collect the right data, in the right amount, using the right methods. The goal is to produce valid and objective conclusions.
Before you run a single test, you need:

A clear research question that can be converted into testable hypotheses (H₀ and Hₐ).
An analysis plan — which variables will you measure? Are they categorical or numeric? What statistical test is appropriate?
A predetermined significance level (usually α = 0.05). Never change this after looking at the data.
Awareness of p-hacking — do not hunt through variables or analyses until you find a significant result. A null result is still a valid and publishable result.


2.1.1 The Three Principles of Efficient Experimental Design







Principle
What it means




Randomisation
Every experimental unit has an equal chance of being assigned to any treatment group. This eliminates selection bias.


Blocking
Group similar experimental units together before randomising. This reduces the effect of known sources of variability (similar to stratified random sampling).


Replication
Repeat each experimental condition independently so that variability can be estimated. Large samples reduce random variation and let us detect small differences.

title: Week 4
text:
2.2 Experimental Studies vs. Observational Studies








Feature
Experimental Study
Observational Study




Researcher role
Intervenes / manipulates conditions
Only observes existing conditions


Randomisation
Possible
Not possible


Confounding
Can be controlled via design
Must be controlled statistically


Example
Clinical trial (drug vs placebo)
Survey on coffee and heart disease









TipQuick Rule



If you are unsure whether a study is experimental or observational, ask: Did the researcher set the conditions under which the data were collected? If yes → experimental. If no → observational.



2.2.1 Two Sources of Problems in Observational Studies

Non-random selection — If participants are not selected randomly, the sample may not represent the population.
Uncontrolled extraneous variables — Variables you did not account for that affect the outcome.

title: Week 4
text:
2.3 Association vs. Causation

“Association is not causation.”

Two variables are associated (or correlated) when they move together in a systematic way — as X increases, Y tends to increase (or decrease). However, this does not mean X causes Y.

2.3.1 Types of Association








Type
Description
Example




Statistical association
Variables are correlated; no causation implied
X and Y are correlated


Causal association
One variable directly causes a change in the other
Sugar consumption → tooth decay


Non-causal association
A third variable explains the relationship between X and Y
Confounding (see below)


Chance association (spurious)
The relationship is a coincidence
Ice cream sales & drownings — both rise in summer due to weather





2.3.2 Common Measures of Association

Pearson’s correlation coefficient (r) — parametric, measures linear association
Spearman’s rank correlation — non-parametric, based on ranked data (uses median)
Chi-square test — for categorical variables
Odds ratio / Relative risk — used in logistic regression

title: Week 4
text:
2.4 Extraneous and Confounding Variables

2.4.1 Extraneous Variables
Variables other than the independent variable that may affect the result. They can be:

Controlled — accounted for in the study design
Uncontrolled — not controlled, and potentially problematic



2.4.2 Confounding Variables
An uncontrolled extraneous variable that:

Influences both the independent and the dependent variable
Makes it impossible to determine which variable is truly causing the observed effect


Key rule: All confounding variables are extraneous variables, but not all extraneous variables are confounding.







NoteExample: Alcohol, Age, and HDL Cholesterol



A study examines the effect of alcohol consumption on HDL (good) cholesterol in males. The observed association could be confounded by age — because age independently affects cholesterol levels. We cannot separate the effect of alcohol from the effect of age.
In a linear model, age would be included and held constant to control for this confounding effect:
\[\text{HDL} = \alpha + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Alcohol} + \beta_2 \cdot \text{Age} + \varepsilon\]




2.4.3 Spurious Correlation
A spurious correlation is when two variables appear associated but there is no logical or causal link — the association is a coincidence, often caused by a hidden third variable.

Classic example: Ice cream sales and drowning rates are both correlated — but both are driven by hot weather, not by each other.

title: Week 4
text:
2.5 Key Terms in Experimental Design







Term
Definition




Experimental unit
The subject/item on which an experiment is performed (e.g., patients, animals, lab samples)


Factor
A controlled explanatory variable whose levels are set by the experimenter


Level
Each distinct setting of a factor (e.g., “Drug A”, “Drug B”, “Drug C”)


Treatment
A specific combination of factor levels applied to an experimental unit


Response variable
The outcome being measured (dependent variable / Y)


Explanatory variable
The variable(s) used to explain the outcome (independent variable / X)









NoteExample: Drug + Vitamin C Study



“Six groups of patients are each treated with one of 3 drugs, either combined with Vitamin C or alone.”

Experimental units: Patients
Factors: Type of drug (3 levels) and Vitamin C (2 levels: yes/no)
Treatments: 6 (= 2 × 3 combinations)
Design: Factorial design → analysed using two-way ANOVA

title: Week 4
text:
2.6 Randomised Designs and Blind Studies

2.6.1 Completely Randomised Design (CRD)
All experimental units are randomly assigned to treatment groups. Every unit has an equal chance of receiving any treatment. This eliminates selection bias.


2.6.2 Randomised Block Design (RBD)
When known differences between units exist (e.g., gender), units are first divided into homogeneous blocks, then randomly assigned to treatment groups within each block. This reduces noise from that known variable.

Complete block: Every block contains every treatment.
Incomplete block: Not all treatments appear in every block (adjusted statistically).



2.6.3 Blind Studies (Reducing Bias Further)







Design
Who doesn’t know the treatment assignment




Single blind
Participants only


Double blind
Participants and researchers administering the treatment


Triple blind
Participants, researchers, and the data analysts

title: Week 4
text:
2.7 ANOVA — Preview
ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) extends the two-sample t-test to compare two or more means simultaneously.



Type
Independent Variables
Dependent Variable




One-way ANOVA
1
1


Two-way ANOVA
2
1




2.7.1 Hypotheses (e.g., one-way ANOVA with 3 groups)
\[H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3\] \[H_A: \text{at least one mean is different}\]






Warning



ANOVA tells you if a difference exists between groups — not where the difference lies. A post-hoc analysis (e.g., Tukey’s test) is needed to identify which specific groups differ.


ANOVA partitions total variation into:

Explained variation — variation due to the treatment/design
Unexplained variation (error) — inherent variation within groups

title: Week 4
section: 2.8 Effect Size, Sample Size, and Statistical Power
text:
2.8 Effect Size, Sample Size, and Statistical Power
These three quantities are deeply interconnected. Understanding their relationship is essential for designing studies and interpreting results.

2.8.1 Effect Size (Cohen’s d)
Effect size measures the practical significance of a difference — how large the difference between two means is relative to the variability in the data.
\[d = \frac{\mu_A - \mu_0}{\sigma}\]
Where:

\(\mu_A\) = mean under the alternative hypothesis
\(\mu_0\) = mean under the null hypothesis
\(\sigma\) = population standard deviation

A larger effect size = easier to detect a real difference.


2.8.2 Statistical Power (1 − β)
Power is the probability of correctly rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually false.
\[\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false})\]

Low power → high risk of a Type II error (missing a real effect)
Target power is typically 80% or 90% in practice



2.8.3 The Relationship Between Effect Size, Sample Size, and Power



If you…
Then power…




Increase sample size
Increases


Increase effect size
Increases


Decrease variability (σ)
Increases


Use a one-tailed instead of two-tailed test
Increases


Decrease α (e.g., from 0.05 to 0.01)
Decreases









TipThe key insight from the simulation exercise



As sample size increases, sample means converge to the true population means, and p-values generally decrease — making it easier to detect a true difference. However, with a very small effect size, you may need an enormous sample to achieve significance.

title: Week 4
section: 4.2 Power Analysis in R — pwr Package
text:
4.2 Power Analysis in R — pwr Package
install.packages("pwr")
library(pwr)

4.2.1 Key pwr Functions



Function
Use case




pwr.t.test
One sample, two sample, or paired t-test


pwr.anova.test
Balanced one-way ANOVA


pwr.chisq.test
Chi-square test


pwr.2p.test
Two proportions (equal n)


pwr.r.test
Correlation


pwr.f2.test
General linear model



For each function you provide three of: sample size, effect size, significance level, power — and the fourth is calculated.


4.2.2 Worked Example: Chemical Company
A government inspector tests whether a chemical product contains more than 4.0 ml of caustic material (the safe limit). She takes n = 30 samples; σ = 1.3; the true mean may be as high as 4.35.
Effect size:
\[d = \frac{4.35 - 4.0}{1.3} = 0.2692\]
Power calculation in R:
pwr.t.test(n = 30, d = 0.2692, sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "one.sample", alternative = "greater")
Result: Power = 0.419 → only a 41.9% chance of detecting the effect with this sample size. The sample is underpowered.

title: Week 4
text:
4.3 Exercise 1: Height Study
Scenario: Testing whether the mean height of students at CIT exceeds 160 cm.
\[H_0: \mu = 160 \text{ cm}, \quad H_A: \mu > 160 \text{ cm}\]
Sample: n = 25, \(\bar{x}\) = 173.4 cm, s = 9
Type I Error: Reject H₀ and conclude that mean height > 160 cm when it is not.
Type II Error: Fail to reject H₀ and conclude mean height ≤ 160 cm when it actually is greater.
Power (if true μ = 165):
pwr.t.test(n = 25, d = 5/9, sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "one.sample", alternative = "greater")
# Power ≈ 85%
Two-tailed version (same μ = 165):
pwr.t.test(n = 25, d = 5/9, sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "one.sample")
# Power ≈ 76%

Conclusion: A one-tailed test is more powerful than a two-tailed test for a given effect size, sample size, and α — because all of the rejection region is on one side.

title: Week 4
text:
4.4 Exercise 2: Light Bulb Lifespan
Scenario: A manufacturer claims bulbs last 850 hours on average (σ = 50). A consumer group believes the true mean is only 810 hours.
\[H_0: \mu = 850, \quad H_A: \mu < 850\]
Required sample size for 90% power:
pwr.t.test(d = (810 - 850) / 50, power = 0.9,
           sig.level = 0.05, type = "one.sample",
           alternative = "less")
# n ≈ 15 (round up)
Simulation to verify (10,000 tests):
pvalues <- rep(0, 10000)
for (i in 1:10000) {
  sA <- rnorm(15, 810, 50)
  pvalues[i] <- t.test(sA, y = NULL, "less", 850)$p.value
}
sum(pvalues < 0.05)   # should be approximately 9,000 (90%)

title: Week 5 – One-Way ANOVA
text: 📝 Study Notes — These notes are designed to be reviewed and expanded. Use the comment spaces and <!-- TODO --> markers to add your own annotations as you revisit this material.

title: Week 5 – One-Way ANOVA
section: 2.1 What is ANOVA and Why Do We Use It?
text:
2.1 What is ANOVA and Why Do We Use It?
ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) is an inferential statistical method used to compare the means of three or more groups simultaneously. It is a direct extension of the independent t-test — whereas the t-test compares exactly two means, ANOVA can handle any number of groups.
Why not just run multiple t-tests?
If you have three groups (e.g. Sand, Clay, Loam), you could technically run three separate t-tests: Sand vs Clay, Sand vs Loam, and Clay vs Loam. However, each test carries a 5% chance of a Type I error (incorrectly rejecting a true null hypothesis). Running multiple tests multiplies this risk — by the time you have run all pairwise comparisons, your real chance of a false positive is much higher than 5%. ANOVA solves this by testing all group means simultaneously while keeping the overall Type I error rate at 5%.

Key Idea: ANOVA compares two sources of variation — variation between groups (due to the treatment/factor) and variation within groups (due to random error). If the between-group variation is much larger than the within-group variation, it suggests that the groups genuinely differ.

title: Week 5 – One-Way ANOVA
text:
2.2 The F-Statistic
The test statistic in ANOVA is the F-statistic (or F-ratio), defined as:
\[F = \frac{\text{Mean Square Between (MSF)}}{\text{Mean Square Within (MSE)}}\]

If the factor has no effect, the between-group variance and within-group variance should be roughly equal → F ≈ 1
If the factor has a strong effect, the between-group variance will be much larger → F >> 1

The F-statistic follows an F-distribution under the null hypothesis, and significance is assessed either by comparing F to a critical value from the F-table, or by computing a p-value.

title: Week 5 – One-Way ANOVA
text:
2.3 Sum of Squares Decomposition
The total variation in the data is partitioned into two components:
\[SS_T = SS_F + SS_E\]








Component
Name
Measures




\(SS_T\)
Total Sum of Squares
Total variation in all observations


\(SS_F\)
Factor (Between) Sum of Squares
Variation due to differences between group means


\(SS_E\)
Error (Within) Sum of Squares
Variation due to random error within groups




2.3.1 Formulas
Total Sum of Squares: \[SS_T = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} \left(y_{ij} - \bar{\bar{y}}\right)^2\]
Factor Sum of Squares: \[SS_F = n \sum_{i} \left(\bar{y}_i - \bar{\bar{y}}\right)^2\]
Error Sum of Squares: \[SS_E = SS_T - SS_F\]
Where:

\(y_{ij}\) = observation \(j\) in treatment group \(i\)
\(\bar{y}_i\) = mean of group \(i\)
\(\bar{\bar{y}}\) = grand mean (overall mean of all observations)
\(n\) = number of replicas per group

Results are always presented in a standard ANOVA table:

The p-value of 0.025 < 0.05 → we reject H₀. At least one soil type produces a significantly different mean yield.

title: Week 5 – One-Way ANOVA
text:
2.5 Hypotheses
\[H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 \quad \text{(all population means are equal)}\]
\[H_A: \text{at least one group mean differs}\]

Note: The alternative hypothesis is phrased as “at least one mean differs,” NOT “all means differ.” This matters when interpreting results — ANOVA only tells you that a difference exists somewhere, not where.

title: Week 5 – One-Way ANOVA
text:
2.6 Assumptions of ANOVA
Before running ANOVA, you must verify these four assumptions:

Random sampling — observations must be randomly sampled from each population.
Independence — observations within and between groups must be independent.
Normality of residuals — the error terms should be normally distributed (check with Q-Q plot or Shapiro-Wilk test).
Homogeneity of variance (homoscedasticity) — the variance should be approximately equal across all groups.

Tests for equal variance:

Fligner-Killeen test (robust to non-normality, recommended)
Levene’s test (from the car package in R)
Bartlett’s test (assumes normality)

For the soil data, the Fligner-Killeen test gave p = 0.833 → we fail to reject the null hypothesis of equal variances → ANOVA is appropriate.

title: Week 5 – One-Way ANOVA
text:
2.7 ANOVA as a Linear Model
ANOVA is a special case of a linear model. The effects model is:
\[y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}\]
Where:

\(\mu\) = overall (grand) population mean
\(\tau_i\) = treatment effect = difference between group \(i\)’s mean and the grand mean
\(\varepsilon_{ij}\) = random error term (assumed iid Normal with mean 0 and constant variance)


2.7.1 Dummy Variables
Since soil type is a categorical variable, R encodes it using dummy variables. With \(k\) groups, R creates \(k - 1\) dummy variables and designates one group as the reference (baseline).
For our 3 soil types (baseline = Clay):



Observation
\(x_1\) (Loam)
\(x_2\) (Sand)




Clay
0
0


Loam
1
0


Sand
0
1



The fitted model becomes:
\[\hat{y}_{ij} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_1 + \hat{\beta}_2 x_2\]
\[\hat{y}_{ij} = 11.5 + 2.8\, x_1 - 1.6\, x_2\]
Where \(\hat{\beta}_0 = 11.5\) is the mean yield for Clay (baseline), \(+2.8\) is the difference between Loam and Clay, and \(-1.6\) is the difference between Sand and Clay.


2.7.2 Treatment Effects



Soil Type
Treatment Effect (\(\hat{\tau}_i\))
Interpretation

Slightly below grand mean


Loam
+2.4
Above grand mean (highest yield)


Sand

Below grand mean (lowest yield)

title: Week 5 – One-Way ANOVA
text:
2.8 Effect Size: Eta-Squared (η²)
η² (Eta-squared) measures the proportion of total variance in the response variable explained by the factor. It is directly analogous to R² in regression.
\[\eta^2 = \frac{SS_F}{SS_T} = \frac{99.2}{414.7} \approx 0.239\]
Interpretation: 23.9% of the variation in crop yield is explained by soil type. The remaining 76.1% is due to other factors (unexplained variation).

title: Week 5 – One-Way ANOVA
text:
2.9 Post-Hoc Analysis: Tukey’s HSD
ANOVA tells us that at least one group mean differs — it does not tell us which groups differ. We use Tukey’s Honest Significant Difference (HSD) test for pairwise comparisons.
Results for soil data:



Comparison
Difference
95% CI
p-value




Loam − Clay
2.8

0.179

−1.6

−4.4
(−8.19, −0.61)

Only the Sand vs Loam comparison is statistically significant (p = 0.020). The confidence interval for that pair does not include 0, confirming a genuine difference.

💡 Tip: When interpreting pairwise CIs graphically, if a confidence interval crosses zero, the difference is not significant. If it doesn’t cross zero, the difference is significant.

title: Week 5 – One-Way ANOVA
text:
2.10 Model Validation
After running ANOVA, check the model assumptions using diagnostic plots (plot(model)):







Plot
What to Look For




Residuals vs Fitted
Residuals should be randomly scattered around 0 with no patterns


Normal Q-Q Plot
Points should follow the diagonal line; departures suggest non-normality


Scale-Location Plot
Horizontal band of points indicates constant variance


Cook’s Distance
Large values (> 0.5 or 1) flag influential/outlier observations



For the soil data, diagnostics showed no serious violations — a couple of points (6, 11, 13) slightly deviated from the Q-Q line but this was not concerning.

title: Week 5 – One-Way ANOVA
text:
2.11 Non-Parametric Alternatives
If ANOVA assumptions are violated (especially equal variance or normality):








Method
Use When
R Function

Variances differ, or data is not normally distributed; tests equality of medians
kruskal.test()

title: Week 5 – One-Way ANOVA
text:
4.1 The Data
An agricultural researcher measured crop yield (per unit area) from 10 randomly selected fields for each of three soil types.
# soil_data.txt — wide format
Sand   Clay   Loam
   6     17     13
  10     15     16
   8      3      9
   6     11     12
  14     14     15
  17     12     16
   9     12     17
  11      8     13
   7     10     18
  11     13     14
Group means: Sand = 9.9, Clay = 11.5, Loam = 14.3
Grand mean: \(\bar{\bar{y}}\) = 11.9

title: Week 5 – One-Way ANOVA
text:
4.2 R Code Walkthrough
library(tidyverse)
library(car)

# --- 1. Import and reshape data ---
results <- read_tsv("soil_data.txt")

# Convert from wide to long format
results <- results |>
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(),
               names_to = "Soil",
               values_to = "Yield")

# --- 2. Descriptive statistics ---
results |>
  group_by(Soil) |>
  summarise(mean_yield = mean(Yield),
            sd_yield   = sd(Yield),
            var_yield  = var(Yield))

# --- 3. Visualise ---
boxplot(Yield ~ Soil, data = results,
        main = "Crop Yield by Soil Type",
        xlab = "Soil Type", ylab = "Yield")

# --- 4. Check homogeneity of variance ---
fligner.test(Yield ~ Soil, data = results)   # p = 0.833 → equal variances OK
leveneTest(Yield ~ Soil, data = results)     # p = 0.781 → consistent result

# --- 5. Ensure Soil is a factor ---
results$Soil <- as.factor(results$Soil)
levels(results$Soil)    # "Clay" "Loam" "Sand"
nlevels(results$Soil)   # 3

# --- 6. Fit one-way ANOVA ---
model_treat <- aov(Yield ~ Soil, data = results)
summary(model_treat)
# Soil:  df=2, SS=99.2, MS=49.60, F=4.245, p=0.025 *

# --- 7. Treatment contrasts (baseline = Clay) ---
summary.lm(model_treat)
# Intercept (Clay):  11.5
# Loam - Clay:       +2.8  (p = 0.078)
# Sand - Clay:       -1.6  (p = 0.305)

# --- 8. Effect size η² ---
# η² = SS_F / SS_T = 99.2 / 414.7 = 0.239

# --- 9. Post-hoc pairwise comparisons ---
TukeyHSD(model_treat)
# Sand-Loam: diff = -4.4, p = 0.020  ← SIGNIFICANT

# --- 10. Model diagnostics ---
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model_treat)

# --- 11. Relevel / change reference group ---
results$Soil <- relevel(results$Soil, "Sand")
model_sand   <- aov(Yield ~ Soil, data = results)
summary.lm(model_sand)

# --- 12. Sum contrasts (compare each group to grand mean) ---
contrasts(results$Soil) <- contr.sum
model_sum <- aov(Yield ~ Soil, data = results)
summary.lm(model_sum)
# Intercept = grand mean (11.9)
# Soil1 = Clay - grand mean = -0.4
# Soil2 = Loam - grand mean = +2.4

# --- 13. Treatment effects table ---
model.tables(model_sum)
# Clay: -0.4 | Loam: +2.4 | Sand: -2.0

# --- 14. Reset contrasts to default ---
contrasts(results$Soil) <- NULL
results$Soil <- relevel(results$Soil, "Clay")

# --- 15. F critical value and p-value (manual check) ---
qf(0.95, df1 = 2, df2 = 27)          # ≈ 3.35 (critical value)
pf(4.245, df1 = 2, df2 = 27,
   lower.tail = FALSE)                 # ≈ 0.025

title: Week 5 – One-Way ANOVA
text:
4.3 Reporting the Results

A one-way analysis of variance indicated that soil type significantly affected crop yield, F(2, 27) = 4.245, MSE = 11.69, p = .025, η² = 0.239. Tukey’s HSD post-hoc test revealed that the mean yield on loam soil was significantly greater than on sand soil (p = .020). No other pairwise differences were statistically significant.

title: Week 6 – Two-Way ANOVA
text: 📝 Note to self: These notes are a first draft — leave room below each section for your own annotations, worked examples, and exam tips.

title: Week 6 – Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.1 What is Two-Way ANOVA?
ANOVA stands for Analysis of Variance. It is a statistical test used to compare the means of three or more groups to see if at least one group mean is significantly different from the others.
One-Way ANOVA (covered previously) uses a single categorical independent variable to explain differences in a continuous dependent variable.
Two-Way ANOVA extends this idea to include two categorical independent variables (called factors) and one continuous dependent variable. This lets us ask three questions at once:

Does Factor A affect the outcome?
Does Factor B affect the outcome?
Do Factors A and B interact — does the effect of one depend on the level of the other?


💡 Key insight from class: Always look at the interaction effect first. If there is a significant interaction, interpreting the main effects in isolation is not meaningful.

title: Week 6 – Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.2 Main Effects vs Interaction Effects
Main effects are the individual effects of each factor, considered separately, averaged across all levels of the other factor.
Interaction effects describe whether the effect of one factor depends on the level of another factor. This is the most important thing to examine in a two-way ANOVA.
For example: Does the effect of pH on product viscosity depend on the level of catalyst concentration being used? If yes — there is an interaction.

💡 Simple rule: If the interaction is significant, you cannot meaningfully talk about the main effects in isolation. You must instead look at simple effects (see Section 2.7).

title: Week 6 – Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.3 The Three Null Hypotheses
A two-way ANOVA tests three separate null hypotheses simultaneously:








Hypothesis
Null (H₀)
Alternative (H₁)




Factor A (main effect)
Population means are equal for all levels of Factor A
At least one group mean differs


Factor B (main effect)
Population means are equal for all levels of Factor B
At least one group mean differs


Interaction A×B
No interaction between Factor A and Factor B
There is a significant interaction




2.3.1 Extension: How Many Hypotheses in a Three-Way ANOVA?
For a three-way ANOVA, the answer is 7:

3 hypotheses for the main effects (one per factor)
3 hypotheses for the two-factor interactions (A×B, A×C, B×C)
1 hypothesis for the three-factor interaction (A×B×C)

title: Week 6 – Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.4 Factorial Design
To properly test for an interaction effect, the experiment must use a factorial design. This means every possible combination of factor levels must appear in the data.

If Factor A has a levels and Factor B has b levels, the number of treatment combinations is:

\[
\text{Total combinations} = a \times b
\]

Each complete set of all \(ab\) combinations is called a replicate (\(r\)).
Total number of observations = \(r \times a \times b\)

title: Week 6 – Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.5 The Effects Model
The statistical model for two-way ANOVA is written as:
\[
y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + e_{ijk}
\]







Term
Meaning




\(y_{ijk}\)
The observed value for replicate \(k\), at level \(i\) of Factor A and level \(j\) of Factor B


\(\mu\)
The overall (grand) mean


\(\alpha_i\)
The treatment effect of Factor A at level \(i\)


\(\beta_j\)
The treatment effect of Factor B at level \(j\)


\((\alpha\beta)_{ij}\)
The interaction effect between Factor A and Factor B


\(e_{ijk}\)
The random error term, assumed \(e_{ijk} \sim \text{NID}(0, \sigma^2)\)



The subscript \(k\) is the new addition compared to one-way ANOVA — it represents the replicates.

2.5.1 Hypotheses in Effects Model Notation
\[
H_0: \alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \ldots = \alpha_a = 0 \quad \text{(no effect of Factor A)}
\] \[
H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \ldots = \beta_b = 0 \quad \text{(no effect of Factor B)}
\] \[
H_0: (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0 \text{ for all } i,j \quad \text{(no interaction)}
\]

title: Week 6 – Two-Way ANOVA
section: 2.6 Sums of Squares and the ANOVA Table
text:
2.6 Sums of Squares and the ANOVA Table
The total variance in the dataset is partitioned into four parts:
\[
SS_T = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_E
\]










Source
Sum of Squares
Degrees of Freedom
Mean Square
F-ratio




Factor A
\(SS_A\)
\(a-1\)
\(MS_A = \frac{SS_A}{a-1}\)
\(\frac{MS_A}{MS_E}\)


Factor B
\(SS_B\)
\(b-1\)
\(MS_B = \frac{SS_B}{b-1}\)
\(\frac{MS_B}{MS_E}\)


Interaction AB
\(SS_{AB}\)
\((a-1)(b-1)\)
\(MS_{AB} = \frac{SS_{AB}}{(a-1)(b-1)}\)
\(\frac{MS_{AB}}{MS_E}\)


Error
\(SS_E\)
\(ab(n-1)\)
\(MS_E = \frac{SS_E}{ab(n-1)}\)
—


Total
\(SS_T\)
\(abn-1\)
—
—



Each F-ratio is compared against a critical value (or produces a p-value) to determine statistical significance.
The full formulas for each sum of squares are:
\[
SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{b}\sum_{k=1}^{n}(y_{ijk} - \bar{y}_{\cdots})^2
\] \[
SS_A = nb\sum_{i=1}^{a}(\bar{y}_{i\cdot\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdots})^2
\] \[
SS_B = na\sum_{j=1}^{b}(\bar{y}_{\cdot j\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdots})^2
\] \[
SS_{AB} = n\sum_{j=1}^{b}\sum_{i=1}^{a}(\bar{y}_{ij\cdot} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot j\cdot} + \bar{y}_{\cdots})^2
\] \[
SS_E = \sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{b}\sum_{k=1}^{n}(y_{ijk} - \bar{y}_{ij\cdot})^2
\]

title: Week 6 – Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.7 Interaction Plots
Before running the model, it is good practice to plot the data to visually check for an interaction. An interaction plot shows the mean of the dependent variable for each combination of factor levels, with lines connecting levels of one factor across levels of the other.
Reading interaction plots:

Parallel lines → No interaction. The effect of one factor is the same regardless of the level of the other.
Non-parallel (crossing or diverging) lines → Interaction present. The effect of one factor changes depending on the level of the other.

section: 2.8 Practical Example: Viscosity, pH, and Catalyst Concentration
text:
2.8 Practical Example: Viscosity, pH, and Catalyst Concentration
An experiment was conducted to investigate whether pH and Catalyst Concentration affect the Viscosity of a product. The design used:

Factor A (pH): 2 levels — 5.6 and 5.9
Factor B (Catalyst Concentration): 2 levels — 2.5 and 2.7
3 replicates per treatment combination → 12 total observations


2.8.1 Data Summary



pH
Catalyst Conc
Viscosity Readings
Treatment Mean




5.6
2.5
192, 199, 193
194.67


5.6
2.7
178, 185, 188
183.67


5.9
2.5
185, 193, 190
189.33


5.9
2.7
197, 196, 204
199.00



Grand mean \(\bar{y}_{\cdots} = 191.67\)


2.8.2 R Output — Two-Way ANOVA
           Df  Sum Sq  Mean Sq  F value  Pr(>F)
Catalyst    1     1.3      1.3    0.070  0.79778
pH          1    75.0     75.0    3.947  0.08218 .
Catalyst:pH 1   320.3    320.3   16.860  0.00341 **
Residuals   8   152.0     19.0

Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1


2.8.3 Interpretation

Interaction term (Catalyst:pH): Significant at the 1% level (p = 0.003). We reject H₀ for the interaction.
Main effects (Catalyst, pH): Neither is statistically significant on its own.


This tells us: the effect that catalyst concentration has on viscosity depends on the pH level — and vice versa. The main effects alone are misleading here.



2.8.4 Estimated Model Parameters
The estimated effect parameters from the ANOVA effects model:

\(\hat{\alpha}_1 = 189.1667 - 191.6667 = -2.5\) (pH = 5.6)
\(\hat{\alpha}_2 = 194.1667 - 191.6667 = 2.5\) (pH = 5.9)
\(\hat{\beta}_1 = 192 - 191.6667 = 0.3333\) (Catalyst = 2.5)
\(\hat{\beta}_2 = 191.3333 - 191.6667 = -0.3333\) (Catalyst = 2.7)

Interaction estimates \((\widehat{\alpha\beta})_{ij}\):

For pH = 5.6, Catalyst = 2.5: \(194.6667 - 189.1667 - 192 + 191.6667 = 5.1667\)
For pH = 5.6, Catalyst = 2.7: \(183.6667 - 189.1667 - 191.3333 + 191.6667 = -5.1667\)
For pH = 5.9, Catalyst = 2.5: \(189.3333 - 194.1667 - 192 + 191.6667 = -5.1667\)
For pH = 5.9, Catalyst = 2.7: \(199 - 194.1667 - 191.3333 + 191.6667 = 5.1667\)

Fitted value for pH = 5.6, Catalyst = 2.5:
\[
\hat{y}_{11k} = 191.6667 + (-2.5) + 0.3333 + 5.1667 = 194.6667
\]

title: Week 6 – Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.9 Simple Effects
Since the interaction is significant, we must examine each factor while holding the other factor constant. These are called the simple effects.
We use Tukey’s HSD (Honest Significant Difference) post-hoc test to compare all pairwise group means.

2.9.1 Key Results from Tukey’s HSD
Catalyst effect (across all pH levels):
Difference = −0.667, p = 0.798 → Not significant
pH effect (across all catalyst levels):
Difference = 5.0, p = 0.082 → Not significant
Simple effect — pH, holding Catalyst at 2.5:
pH 5.6 vs 5.9: difference = −5.333, p = 0.481 → Not significant
Simple effect — pH, holding Catalyst at 2.7:
pH 5.6 vs 5.9: difference = 15.333, p = 0.011 → ✅ Significant!

Conclusion: At a catalyst concentration of 2.7, there is a statistically significant difference in mean viscosity between pH 5.6 and pH 5.9 (difference = 15.33, p = 0.011). This is the source of the interaction.


📝 Annotation space: Can you sketch the interaction plot and annotate which crossing of lines corresponds to this significant simple effect?

title: Week 6 – Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.10 Effect Size: Partial Eta Squared
To quantify how much of the variance in the outcome is explained by each factor, we use Partial Eta Squared (\(\eta^2_P\)):
\[
\eta^2_P = \frac{SS_F}{SS_F + SS_E}
\]
Where \(SS_F\) is the sum of squares for the factor of interest, and \(SS_E\) is the error sum of squares.

2.10.1 Example — Effect of pH on Viscosity
\[
\eta^2_P = \frac{SS_A}{SS_A + SS_E} = \frac{75}{75 + 152} = 0.33 \approx 33\%
\]
We can say that 33% of the variation in viscosity is associated with differences in pH level (after accounting for the other factor).

⚠️ Note: \(\eta^2_P\) can be biased (overestimate effect size) in small samples.

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
This week covered two main areas:

Multiple Comparisons — how to fairly compare more than two group means after a significant ANOVA result
Two-Way ANOVA — extending one-way ANOVA to handle two independent variables simultaneously, including their interaction effect
Factorial Design — the correct experimental design when testing for interactions
Effect Size — measuring how much of the variance is explained by each factor (partial η²)
Model Validation in R — checking ANOVA assumptions with diagnostic plots
Lab Practice — running one-way ANOVA by hand in Excel and in R, including post-hoc tests

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.1 The Multiple Comparisons Problem
When ANOVA tells us that at least one group mean is different, it does not tell us which groups differ. A natural first instinct is to run separate t-tests for every pair of groups — but this creates a serious problem.
Every individual test carries a risk (α, usually 5%) of a Type I error (falsely concluding there is a difference). When you run many tests on the same data, those error risks stack up. This inflated overall error rate is called the Family-Wise Error Rate (FWER).

Example: Testing 3 soil types (Sand, Clay, Loam) means 3 pairwise comparisons.
With 6 cities (from the New Car Interest Rate dataset), there are 15 pairwise comparisons — the chance of at least one false positive becomes very large.


2.1.1 Post-Hoc Tests
Post-hoc (after-the-fact) tests are designed to run all pairwise comparisons while controlling the FWER at the chosen α level. They are more conservative than plain t-tests.








Method
How it works
When to use




Tukey’s HSD (Honest Significant Difference)
Controls FWER by adjusting critical values
Most common; good general choice


Bonferroni
Divides α by the number of comparisons
Simple; conservative with many comparisons


Separate t-tests
No adjustment
Avoid — inflated Type I error





2.1.2 Worksheet 4 Results (Soil Yield Data)



Comparison
Separate t-tests
Tukey’s HSD
Bonferroni




Loam – Clay
0.081
0.179
0.234


Loam – Sand
0.006
0.020
0.023


Clay – Sand
0.351
0.555
0.914




Key takeaway: Only the Loam vs. Sand comparison is statistically significant.
Notice how the adjusted p-values (Tukey, Bonferroni) are larger than the raw t-test p-values — this is the conservative adjustment at work.

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.2 Two-Way ANOVA

2.2.1 What is it?
One-way ANOVA tested one independent variable (e.g., soil type) against one continuous dependent variable (yield).
Two-way ANOVA tests two independent (categorical) variables simultaneously against one continuous dependent variable. This is powerful because it also reveals whether the two factors interact.


2.2.2 Main Effects vs. Interaction Effect

Main effect of Factor A — the overall effect of Factor A on the response, ignoring Factor B.
Main effect of Factor B — the overall effect of Factor B on the response, ignoring Factor A.
Interaction effect (A × B) — does the effect of Factor A depend on which level of Factor B we’re at? This is the most important question in two-way ANOVA.


Always check the interaction effect first. If it is significant, the main effects cannot be interpreted in isolation — you must look at them within each level of the other factor (called simple effects).



2.2.3 The Three Null Hypotheses
For a two-way ANOVA with factors A and B:

H₀ (Factor A): All population means for Factor A are equal (no main effect of A)
H₀ (Factor B): All population means for Factor B are equal (no main effect of B)
H₀ (Interaction): The effect of Factor A is the same at all levels of Factor B (no interaction)


Thinking further: A three-way ANOVA has 7 hypotheses — 3 main effects + 3 two-way interactions + 1 three-way interaction.



2.2.4 The Two-Way ANOVA Effects Model
\[Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}\]







Symbol
Meaning




\(Y_{ijk}\)
Response variable (observation \(k\) at level \(i\) of A and level \(j\) of B)


\(\mu\)
Overall population mean


\(\alpha_i\)
Treatment effect of Factor A at level \(i\)


\(\beta_j\)
Treatment effect of Factor B at level \(j\)


\((\alpha\beta)_{ij}\)
Interaction effect


\(\varepsilon_{ijk}\)
Residual / error term


\(k\)
Replicate index

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.3 Factorial Design
To test for an interaction effect, you must use a factorial (fully crossed) design:

“All possible combinations of all treatment levels must be included for every replicate.”

If Factor A has a levels and Factor B has b levels:
\[\text{Number of treatment combinations} = a \times b\]
With \(r\) replicates:
\[\text{Total observations} = r \times a \times b\]

Example (Viscosity study):
pH has 2 levels (5.6, 5.9) × Catalyst concentration has 2 levels (2.5, 2.7) = 4 combinations.
With 3 replicates → 12 total viscosity measurements.

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.4 Interaction Plots
An interaction plot graphs the mean response for each combination of factor levels. It is the clearest way to visually assess whether an interaction exists.







Plot appearance
Conclusion




Lines are approximately parallel
No interaction between factors


Lines cross or diverge sharply
Interaction is likely present




Tip: Don’t overcomplicate it. Pick one line, trace how the response changes as the other factor changes — then repeat for the other line. If the direction or magnitude of change differs between lines, that’s an interaction.

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.7 Simple Effects (Post-Hoc for Two-Way ANOVA)
If the interaction is significant, examine simple effects: the effect of one factor while holding the other factor constant at each level.

Viscosity Example: The interaction was significant (p = 0.03).
- Effect of pH (5.6 vs 5.9) when catalyst = 2.5: not significant
- Effect of pH (5.6 vs 5.9) when catalyst = 2.7: significant (difference = 15, p = 0.01)

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.8 Effect Size — Partial Eta Squared (η²ₚ)
To measure how much of the variance is explained by each factor:
\[\eta^2_p = \frac{SS_{factor}}{SS_{factor} + SS_{error}}\]
This is analogous to R² in regression. It tells you the proportion of variance in the response explained by a given factor, after removing variance accounted for by the other variables.

Interpretation example: η²ₚ = 0.33 for pH means 33% of the variation in viscosity is associated with pH.

title: Week 7 Study Guide: Multiple Comparisons & Two-Way ANOVA
text:
2.9 ANOVA Assumptions (Checklist)
Before trusting ANOVA results, verify:

Random sampling — ensured at data collection stage
Equal variances (homogeneity) — test with Levene’s test or Bartlett’s test
Independence of error terms — observations should not influence each other
Normality of residuals — check with QQ-plot and/or Shapiro-Wilk test


If equal variance assumption is violated → use the non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis test (uses ranks/medians instead of means).

Identifying and handling missing data in R
Visualising missing data patterns using the VIM and summarytools packages
Strategies for dealing with missing data: removal vs. imputation
Iterative imputation with MICE and irmi()
