title: Week 3 Study Guide: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
2.1 Core Explanation

2.1.1 What Is Missing Data?
In real-world datasets, it is very common to find that some values are simply not recorded — these are called missing values, represented in R as NA (Not Available). Missing data can arise for many reasons: a participant dropped out of a study, a sensor failed to record, or a question was left blank on a survey.
Before doing any analysis, it is important to understand how much data is missing and where it is missing from, because this can significantly affect your results.


2.1.2 The Sleep Dataset
The examples in this week’s class used the sleep dataset — a study of sleep patterns across 39 mammal species (62 observations, 10 variables). This dataset was chosen because it has a realistic spread of missing values across several columns.
The variables in the dataset are:



Variable
Description




BodyWgt
Body weight (kg)


BrainWgt
Brain weight (kg)


NonD
Non-dream sleep (hours/day)


Dream
Dream sleep (hours/day)


Sleep
Total sleep (NonD + Dream)


Span
Max life span (years)


Gest
Gestation time (days)


Pred
Predation index (danger from predators)


Exp
Sleep exposure index


Danger
Overall danger index (1 = least, 5 = most)





2.1.3 Basic R Functions for Missing Data
When you only have a few missing values and no obvious patterns, R provides several straightforward tools:
is.na(x)               # Returns TRUE/FALSE for each value — is it missing?
which(is.na(x))        # Tells you the *position* (index) of the NAs
na.rm = TRUE           # Used inside functions like mean() to ignore NAs
complete.cases(x)      # Returns TRUE for rows with no missing values
na.omit(x)             # Removes all rows that contain any NA
Removing rows based on conditions:
# Remove rows where a specific column has NA
df <- df[!is.na(df$specific_column), ]

# Remove rows where EITHER col1 OR col2 has NA
df <- df[!(is.na(df$col1) | is.na(df$col2)), ]


2.1.4 Summarising Your Data with summarytools
The summarytools package provides a quick way to see the distribution of each variable and spot where values are missing. It produces a Data Frame Summary table showing the mean, standard deviation, min/max, and the number of distinct valid values for each column.
library(summarytools)
dfSummary(sleep)
This is a good first step — it gives you a bird’s-eye view of the entire dataset, including which variables have fewer valid values (a sign of missingness).

title: Week 3 Study Guide: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
2.2 Visualising Missing Data
The VIM package provides powerful tools specifically for exploring missing data visually. The key principle is that red = missing and blue = complete across all VIM plots.

2.2.1 aggr() — Count and Plot Missingness
library(VIM)
aggr(sleep, numbers = TRUE, prop = FALSE)
This produces two plots: 1. A bar chart showing how many values are missing per variable (e.g., NonD had ~14 missing values in the sleep dataset). 2. A combination plot showing which combinations of variables tend to be missing together. For example, rows 31 and 62 both had NonD, Dream, and Sleep missing simultaneously — which makes sense because Sleep = NonD + Dream.


2.2.2 barMiss() — Missingness by a Categorical Variable
barMiss(sleep[, c("Exp", "Sleep")])
Use this when you want to see whether missingness in one variable is related to the values of another variable. For the sleep data, Sleep had 4 missing values: 1 occurred when Exp = 1, and 3 occurred when Exp = 5. Exp itself had no missing values.


2.2.3 histMiss() — Missingness by a Continuous Variable
histMiss(sleep[, c("Span", "Sleep")])
This is the same idea as barMiss() but for continuous variables, where a histogram is more appropriate. For the sleep data, the 4 missing Sleep values appeared in cases where Span was between 10–20 years and 20–30 years.


2.2.4 marginplot() — Comparing Distributions With and Without Missingness
marginplot(sleep[, c("Span", "Sleep")])
This is one of the most informative plots. It shows a scatter plot of two variables, with extra boxplots in the margins:

The red boxplot shows the distribution of Sleep for cases where Span is missing — i.e., what does Sleep look like when we don’t know Span?
The blue boxplot shows the distribution of Sleep for cases where Span is observed.

If the red and blue boxplots look very similar, it suggests the data may be Missing At Random (MAR) — the missingness is not related to the unobserved values. If they look very different, the missing data pattern may be informative.

Annotation space: How did the red and blue boxplots compare in the sleep example? What might that suggest about the missing data mechanism?

title: Week 3 Study Guide: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
2.3 Dealing with Missing Data

2.3.1 Option 1: Complete Case Analysis (Listwise Deletion)
The simplest approach is to just remove any row that contains a missing value. In R’s lm() function, this is actually the default behaviour — it automatically uses only complete cases.
# These two are equivalent for linear regression:
lm(Sleep ~ BodyWgt, data = sleep)
lm(Sleep ~ BodyWgt, data = na.omit(sleep))
Other related options:
na.fail()     # Throws an error if any NAs are present — forces you to address it
na.exclude()  # Like na.omit() but keeps NA slots in predictions
When is this acceptable? Only when the amount of missing data is small and there is no systematic pattern to which cases are missing. If certain types of cases are more likely to be missing, removing them can bias your results.


2.3.2 Option 2: Imputation with irmi() (VIM Package)
Imputation means filling in the missing values with estimated values, rather than throwing the data away.
The irmi() function uses Iterative Robust Model-based Imputation:
library(VIM)
imputed_sleep <- irmi(sleep)
How it works: 1. Take one variable (e.g., NonD) as the response variable. 2. Use all other variables as predictors in a regression model. 3. Use the predicted values from that model to fill in the missing NonD values. 4. Repeat this process, cycling through each variable that has missing values. 5. Continue iterating until the imputed values stabilise.
After imputation, you should compare linear model results before and after imputation to see how much the coefficients changed. In the sleep example, the intercept was slightly larger and the slope slightly less negative after imputation.


2.3.3 Option 3: Multiple Imputation with MICE
The MICE package (Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations) is a more sophisticated approach. Instead of imputing once, it creates multiple complete datasets (e.g., 5), fits a model to each one, and then pools the results.
library(mice)
imputed <- mice(sleep, m = 5, method = "pmm")  # pmm = predictive mean matching
models  <- with(imputed, lm(Sleep ~ BodyWgt))
pooled  <- pool(models)
summary(pooled)
Predictive Mean Matching (PMM) works by finding observed values that are close to the predicted value and using one of those as the imputed value. This keeps imputed values realistic (they come from real observed data).
The predictor matrix in MICE output tells you which variables were used to predict the missing values in each column.
Why does the pooled model have larger standard errors? Because pooling across 5 datasets accounts for the extra uncertainty introduced by the fact that we don’t truly know what the missing values are. This is actually more honest than a single imputation.

Annotation space: When would you choose MICE over irmi()? Think about dataset size, the proportion of missing data, and how much you care about uncertainty quantification.

title: Week 3 Study Guide: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
3.1 Core Explanation

3.1.1 What Is Statistical Power?
When you run a hypothesis test, there are two ways you can be wrong:








Decision
H₀ is True
H₀ is False




Reject H₀
Type I error (probability = α)
✅ Correct! (probability = power = 1 − β)


Fail to reject H₀
✅ Correct! (probability = 1 − α)
Type II error (probability = β)



Statistical power is defined as:
\[\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false})\]
In plain English: power is the probability that your test will correctly detect a real effect when one actually exists. A higher power means a better, more sensitive test.


3.1.2 The Four Interconnected Quantities
Power analysis is built on the relationship between four quantities. If you know any three, you can calculate the fourth:

Sample size \(n\) — larger samples give more power
Effect size — the size of the difference you’re trying to detect (larger effects are easier to detect)
Significance level \(\alpha\) — the threshold for rejecting \(H_0\) (typically 0.05)
Power \(1 - \beta\) — typically you aim for at least 0.80 (80%)


Important caveat: In practice, you cannot know the true effect size — if you already knew the true difference, there would be no need to test! Instead, you define the minimum effect size of practical importance (the smallest difference that would actually matter for your application).

title: Week 3 Study Guide: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
3.2 Key Equations / Formulas

3.2.1 Z-Test Statistic (known population standard deviation)
\[Z_{stat} = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}\]


3.2.2 Minimum Sample Mean to Reject \(H_0\)
\[\bar{X}_{crit} = \mu + \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \times Z_{crit}\]


3.2.3 Power Calculation
Once you have \(\bar{X}_{crit}\), calculate the probability of exceeding it under the alternative hypothesis:
\[\beta = P\left(\bar{X} < \bar{X}_{crit} \mid \mu = \mu_A\right) = P\left(Z < \frac{\bar{X}_{crit} - \mu_A}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}\right)\]
\[\text{Power} = 1 - \beta\]


3.2.4 Sample Size Formula (known σ, from margin of error)
\[n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \times \sigma}{ME}\right)^2\]


3.2.5 Sample Size Formula (unknown σ, using t-distribution)
\[n = \left(\frac{t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \times s}{ME}\right)^2\]
(Note: this requires iteration since \(t\) depends on \(n\))

title: Week 3 Study Guide: Missing Data & Power Analysis
text:
3.3 Practical Example — Chemical Company

3.3.1 Problem Setup
A chemical company claims their product contains on average μ = 4.0 ml of caustic material per litre. A government inspector tests a sample of n = 30 containers and finds: - Sample mean: \(\bar{x} = 4.3\) ml - Known population standard deviation: \(\sigma = 1.3\)


3.3.2 Step 1: State the Hypotheses
Since the product is only dangerous if it contains more than 4.0 ml, this is a one-tailed test:
\[H_0: \mu = 4.0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_A: \mu > 4.0\]


3.3.3 Step 2: Type I and Type II Errors in Context

Type I error: Concluding the mean is greater than 4 ml when it actually isn’t — falsely accusing the company.
Type II error: Concluding the mean is not greater than 4 ml when it actually is — missing a genuine danger.



3.3.4 Step 3: Conduct the Test (α = 0.05)
\[Z_{stat} = \frac{4.3 - 4.0}{1.3 / \sqrt{30}} = \frac{0.3}{0.2374} = 1.264\]

Critical value for a one-tailed test at α = 0.05: \(Z_{crit} = 1.645\)
Since \(Z_{stat} = 1.264 < 1.645\), we fail to reject \(H_0\)
The p-value is 0.103 > 0.05, confirming this conclusion
95% lower bound: 3.910 (we are 95% confident the true mean is above 3.910 ml)

Conclusion: The data does not provide sufficient evidence that the mean caustic level exceeds 4 ml/litre.


3.3.5 Step 4: Calculate the Power of the Test
Now ask: Was our sample large enough to detect a meaningful difference?
Suppose we define a difference of 0.35 ml/litre as practically significant — i.e., we want to detect if the true mean is μ_A = 4.35.
Find the critical sample mean (threshold for rejection):
\[\bar{X}_{crit} = 4.0 + \frac{1.3}{\sqrt{30}} \times 1.645 = 4.0 + 0.39 = 4.39\]
So we only reject \(H_0\) if the sample mean exceeds 4.39.
Calculate β (probability of Type II error):
\[\beta = P(\bar{X} < 4.39 \mid \mu = 4.35) = P\left(Z < \frac{4.39 - 4.35}{1.3/\sqrt{30}}\right) = P(Z < 0.17) = 0.5675\]
Power:
\[\text{Power} = 1 - 0.5675 = \mathbf{0.4325}\]
Interpretation: There is only a 43.25% chance of correctly detecting a 0.35 ml difference with n = 30. This is quite low — the test is underpowered. We would ideally want power ≥ 0.80.

Annotation space: What would you recommend the inspector do? Collect a larger sample? Define a larger minimum effect size? Both options involve trade-offs.

title: Week 3 Study Guide: Missing Data & Power Analysis
section: 3.4 Sample Size from Margin of Error
text:
3.4 Sample Size from Margin of Error
Power analysis is closely linked to confidence intervals. The margin of error (ME) in a confidence interval is:
\[ME = Z_{\alpha/2} \times \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]
Rearranging to find the required sample size for a desired ME:
\[n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \times \sigma}{ME}\right)^2\]
Example: An analyst wants to estimate mean voltage (σ = 22.5 volts) with a margin of error of 5 volts at 95% confidence:
\[n = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 22.5}{5}\right)^2 = (8.82)^2 \approx 78 \text{ observations}\]
When \(\sigma\) is unknown, replace \(Z\) with the \(t\)-distribution critical value and \(\sigma\) with the sample standard deviation \(s\).

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text: 📝 Study Notes — Week 2 These notes are intended as a review and starting point. Leave your own annotations and expansions in the spaces provided below each section.

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.1 Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics
Descriptive statistics are used simply to describe or summarise the data you have observed — for example, calculating the mean or creating a chart.
Inferential statistics go a step further. They use methods such as hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, and regression analysis to draw conclusions about a larger group (a population) based on data collected from a smaller group (a sample). These inferences are grounded in probability theory.

✏️ Your notes here:

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.2 Population vs. Sample







Term
Definition




Population
All individuals under study


Sample
A subset of the population


Parameter
A characteristic of a population (usually unknown)


Statistic
An estimate of a parameter, calculated from the sample



In practice, we almost never have access to the full population. Instead, we collect a sample and use the statistics from that sample to estimate the unknown population parameters.
For a statistic to reliably estimate a parameter, the sample must be collected in an unbiased way — this is where probability sampling comes in.

✏️ Your notes here:

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.3 Probability Sampling
To ensure a sample is representative of the population, we use probability sampling:

Every member of the population has a known, non-zero chance of being selected.
Units are drawn at random.
Statistical inference techniques assume that samples are drawn randomly.

Without random sampling, estimates may be biased and conclusions unreliable.

✏️ Your notes here:

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.4 Point Estimates and Sampling Error
From a random sample, we calculate point estimates — a single value used to estimate a population parameter.

Example: the sample mean \(\bar{x}\) is used to estimate the population mean \(\mu\).

However, there will always be a difference between the estimate and the true parameter. This difference is called sampling error.

2.4.1 Controlling Sampling Error
Key methods to minimise sampling error include:

Increasing sample size — larger samples are more representative and produce smaller errors.
Using random sampling methods (e.g. stratified sampling).
Calculating the Standard Error of the Mean (SEM).
Using Confidence Intervals.


✏️ Your notes here:

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.5 Standard Error of the Mean (SEM)
The Standard Error of the Mean quantifies how much variability we expect in the sample mean from sample to sample.
\[
\text{SEM} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\]
Where:

\(\sigma\) = population standard deviation
\(n\) = sample size

A smaller SEM means a more precise and reliable estimate. As the sample size \(n\) increases, the SEM decreases.

✏️ Your notes here:

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.6 Sampling Distributions
When we take many samples from a population and calculate a statistic (like the mean) for each, the distribution of those statistics is called a sampling distribution.

The sample mean \(\bar{x}\) varies from sample to sample — it is a random variable.
If the underlying population is normally distributed with mean \(\mu\) and standard deviation \(\sigma\):

\[
\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right)
\]

2.6.1 The Central Limit Theorem (CLT)
What if the population is not normally distributed? The Central Limit Theorem tells us:

For large sample sizes (\(n \geq 30\)), the distribution of the sample mean is approximately normal, regardless of the shape of the underlying population.

This is one of the most important results in statistics — it allows us to use normal-distribution methods even when we don’t know the shape of the population.

✏️ Your notes here:

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.7 Confidence Intervals
A confidence interval (CI) is a range of values within which we are highly confident the true population parameter lies.

2.7.1 Logic of a 95% Confidence Interval
With repeated sampling, 95% of calculated \(\bar{x}\) values will be within two standard errors of the true population mean \(\mu\). Turning this around: given a single sample, we are 95% confident that \(\mu\) lies within two standard errors of our calculated \(\bar{x}\).
\[
\bar{x} \pm 2 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\]
Key point: A larger sample size leads to a narrower (more precise) confidence interval.
Confidence intervals are especially useful because they show the range of plausible values for a parameter, not just a yes/no decision. They help distinguish between statistical and practical significance.

✏️ Your notes here:

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.8 Hypothesis Testing
Hypothesis testing is a formal procedure for answering research questions using data (empirical evidence).

2.8.1 Steps in Hypothesis Testing

State the hypotheses (null and alternative).
Choose a significance level (\(\alpha\), e.g. 0.05).
Collect data and compute a test statistic.
Calculate the p-value.
Make a decision: reject or fail to reject \(H_0\).
State your conclusion in terms of the population parameter.


⚠️ Lecturer’s note (from class): There are only two valid conclusions in hypothesis testing: - “We reject the null hypothesis.” - “We fail to reject the null hypothesis.”
Anything else (e.g. “we accept \(H_1\)”) is incorrect.



2.8.2 The Null Hypothesis (\(H_0\))

The null hypothesis is the statement being tested — typically a claim of no difference or no effect.
We assume it is true until the data provides evidence otherwise.
It is always stated using an equality sign.

Example: \(H_0: \mu = 0\)


2.8.3 The Alternative Hypothesis (\(H_1\) or \(H_a\))

The alternative hypothesis represents the researcher’s theory — what they believe to be true.
It contains all values not included in the null hypothesis.
Together, \(H_0\) and \(H_1\) must cover all eventualities.

Example: \(H_1: \mu \neq 0\)

✏️ Your notes here:

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.9 The p-value
The p-value is a probability that measures the strength of evidence against the null hypothesis.

🎵 Classroom rhyme (do not write in exams!): “If p is low, null must go.”


A small p-value → less likely that \(H_0\) is true → stronger evidence against \(H_0\) → in favour of \(H_1\).
Always quote the p-value when reporting your conclusions.
Conclusions should be stated in terms of the population parameter.


✏️ Your notes here:

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.10 Level of Significance (\(\alpha\))
The significance level \(\alpha\) is a threshold set before the test is run. It represents the degree of uncertainty you are willing to accept.



Significance Level
Common Usage




\(\alpha = 0.05\) (5%)
Most common


\(\alpha = 0.01\) (1%)
More stringent


\(\alpha = 0.001\) (0.1%)
Very stringent



Decision rule:

If \(p \leq \alpha\): Reject \(H_0\) — result is statistically significant.
If \(p > \alpha\): Fail to reject \(H_0\) — insufficient evidence to reject.


⚠️ A non-significant result does not prove \(H_0\) is true — it simply means we don’t have enough evidence to reject it.


✏️ Your notes here:

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
section: 2.11 Type I and Type II Errors
text:
2.11 Type I and Type II Errors
Because conclusions in hypothesis testing are based on sample data, errors are possible.









\(H_0\) is actually TRUE
\(H_0\) is actually FALSE




Reject \(H_0\)
❌ Type I Error (\(\alpha\))
✅ Correct (Power)


Fail to Reject \(H_0\)
✅ Correct
❌ Type II Error (\(\beta\))




Type I Error: Rejecting \(H_0\) when it is actually true (a “false positive”). The probability is controlled by \(\alpha\).
Type II Error: Failing to reject \(H_0\) when it is actually false (a “false negative”). The probability is denoted \(\beta\).


✏️ Your notes here:

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.12 Statistical Power
Power is the probability of correctly rejecting \(H_0\) when it is false — i.e. the probability of detecting a real effect.
\[
\text{Power} = 1 - \beta
\]

We want high power (large \(1 - \beta\)) to maximise our chances of detecting a real difference.
We also want small \(\alpha\) to avoid false positives.
There is a trade-off: decreasing \(\alpha\) automatically increases \(\beta\) (and reduces power).
The solution is to choose an appropriate sample size before the study begins, so that both \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are at acceptable levels.


✏️ Your notes here:

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.13 Effect Size
The effect size measures the magnitude or strength of a relationship between two variables, separate from statistical significance.

A result can be statistically significant but have a tiny effect size (practically unimportant).
A result with a large effect size is more likely to be significant.
Associated with each effect size is a standard error (a measure of uncertainty).


2.13.1 Cohen’s d (for t-tests)
\[
d = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_{\text{pooled}}}
\]
The difference between two means divided by the pooled standard deviation.


2.13.2 \(R^2\) (for Linear Regression)
In linear regression, effect size is measured by \(R^2\) (the square of Pearson’s correlation coefficient \(r\)):
\[
R^2 = r^2
\]
\(R^2\) represents the proportion of variation in the response variable explained by the explanatory variable.

✏️ Your notes here:

title: Week 2: Statistical Inference
text:
2.14 Statistical vs. Clinical (Practical) Significance
This is a critical distinction:









Statistical Significance
Clinical/Practical Significance




What it means
Evidence of an effect in the data
The effect is large enough to matter in practice


Determined by
p-value vs. \(\alpha\)
Effect size


Can be misleading when
Sample size is very large (tiny effects become significant)
Sample size is small (real effects may not be detected)



For a result to be practically meaningful, it must be BOTH statistically significant AND have a meaningful effect size.

A large sample can make a trivially small difference statistically significant.
A small sample may miss a clinically important difference.
Confidence intervals help by showing the range of plausible effect sizes — not just whether an effect is “significant”.


💡 Example from class: Blood pressure — even a small reduction can have important clinical implications, even if the effect size is modest.


✏️ Your notes here:

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
Correlation analysis vs. regression analysis
Pearson’s correlation coefficient (linear) vs. Spearman’s rank (non-linear)
Interpreting the correlation coefficient: direction and strength
Simple linear regression model and its equation
Independent (predictor/explanatory) vs. dependent (response) variables
Scatter plots and the importance of visualisation
The line of best fit and the method of least squares
Residuals and model assumptions
Sum of squares: total, regression, and error
Coefficient of determination (\(R^2\))
ANOVA table for regression
Significance testing of the slope (\(\beta_1\))
Diagnostic plots (residuals vs. fitted, QQ plot, Scale–Location, Cook’s Distance)
Log transformation for non-linear data (Example 2 – Decay dataset)

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.1 What Is Regression Analysis?
Regression analysis is a statistical method used to determine and describe the relationship between two or more numeric variables. The key requirement is that the variables must be numeric (quantitative). For example, investigating whether the yield of a chemical process is related to the operating temperature — both are numbers, so regression is appropriate.
There are two closely related concepts introduced this week:

Correlation analysis asks: does a relationship exist between the two variables, and how strong is it?
Regression analysis goes further: what is the nature/equation of that relationship, and can we use it to make predictions?

In practice, correlation is usually examined first as a preliminary step to regression.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.2 Correlation: Pearson’s vs. Spearman’s
Correlation measures the extent to which two variables are related. Relationships can take many mathematical forms (linear, curved, etc.), but in this course we focus exclusively on linear relationships.








Coefficient
Used for
Symbol




Pearson’s r
Linear correlation
\(r\) (sample), \(\rho\) (population)


Spearman’s rank
Non-linear (monotonic) correlation
\(r_s\)




Important: You cannot determine whether a relationship is linear just by looking at the correlation coefficient number alone. You must also produce a scatter plot to visually confirm linearity. Three datasets can have the same \(r\) value but look completely different when plotted (e.g., one linear, one curved, one with an outlier).

The Pearson correlation coefficient \(r\):

Ranges from −1 to +1
+1 = perfect positive linear relationship
−1 = perfect negative linear relationship
0 = no linear relationship

From the value of \(r\), you can determine two things:

Direction — positive (both variables increase together) or negative (one increases as the other decreases)
Strength — the closer the value is to ±1, the stronger the relationship

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.3 The Straight Line Equation
A straight line in mathematics is described by:
\[y = mx + c\]
where \(m\) is the slope (gradient) and \(c\) is the y-intercept.
In statistics, the same concept uses different notation:
\[Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon\]







Symbol
Meaning




\(Y\)
Response (dependent) variable


\(X\)
Explanatory (independent) variable


\(\beta_0\)
Intercept (equivalent to \(c\)) — where the line crosses the y-axis when \(X = 0\)


\(\beta_1\)
Slope (equivalent to \(m\)) — the change in \(Y\) for every one-unit increase in \(X\)


\(\varepsilon\)
Error term (random noise)




Note from class: The intercept is not usually meaningful on its own. The slope is the key parameter to interpret.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.4 Independent vs. Dependent Variables

The independent variable (also called the predictor or explanatory variable) goes on the x-axis. It is the variable we use to predict or explain the other.
The dependent variable (also called the response variable) goes on the y-axis. It responds to changes in the independent variable.

Tip from class: To figure out which is which, put it into a sentence: “Does Y depend on X, or does X depend on Y?” The variable that depends on the other is the dependent variable.
Example (plant data):
Does height depend on age? → Yes → Height = Y (y-axis), Age = X (x-axis)
When there is one independent variable and one dependent variable, this is called simple linear regression.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.5 Scatter Plots
Before doing any analysis, always plot your data. A scatter plot is a graph of all the \((X, Y)\) pairs. It helps you to:

Check whether the relationship looks linear (if not, linear regression is not appropriate)
Estimate the direction (positive or negative slope)
Get a rough idea of strength (how tightly clustered the points are around an imaginary line)

When interpreting a scatter plot, state three things in this order: 1. Linear (or not) 2. Positive / Negative 3. Strength: weak / moderate / strong

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
section: 2.6 The Line of Best Fit & Least Squares
text:
2.6 The Line of Best Fit & Least Squares
Because real data points do not all fall perfectly on a straight line, there are many possible lines we could draw. We need a principled way to choose the best one.
The method used is called the method of least squares. It selects the line that minimises the sum of the squared vertical distances (called residuals or errors) between each observed data point and the line.
Why square the distances? Because some points lie above the line (positive residual) and some below (negative residual). If we just added them up, positives and negatives would cancel out and mislead us. Squaring makes everything positive.
\[\text{Minimise: } \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2\]
where \(e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i\) is the residual for observation \(i\).
The estimated regression equation is then written:
\[\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x\]
where hats (^) denote estimated values.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.7 Interpreting the Slope
The slope \(\hat{\beta}_1\) tells you:

For every one-unit increase in X, Y increases (or decreases) by \(\hat{\beta}_1\) units.

Plant data example:
Fitted model: \(\hat{y} = -1.03 + 3.047x\)
→ For every one additional week of age, plant height increases by 3.047 cm.
The sign of the slope matches the direction of the correlation: positive slope ↔︎ positive correlation.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.8 Residuals and Model Assumptions
A residual is the difference between what was observed and what was predicted by the model:
\[e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i\]
For the regression model to be valid, the residuals (errors) must satisfy these assumptions:

Linearity — the relationship between X and Y is linear
Independence — the errors are independent of each other
Normality — the errors follow a normal distribution (mean = 0)
Constant variance (homoscedasticity) — the spread of residuals is roughly the same across all fitted values

These are checked using diagnostic plots in R.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.9 Diagnostic Plots in R
Running plot(your_model) produces four plots:







Plot
What it checks




Residuals vs. Fitted
Constant variance — should show no pattern


Normal Q-Q Plot
Normality of residuals — points should fall on the diagonal line


Scale–Location
Another view of constant variance


Cook’s Distance
Influential observations — values > 1 are concerning



You can also use qqPlot(your_model) from the car package for a Q-Q plot with confidence bands.

Exam tip (from lecturer): These diagnostic plots are likely to appear in the terminal exam. Make sure you can interpret each one.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.10 Variation and \(R^2\) (Coefficient of Determination)
The total variation in Y can be split into two parts:
\[SS_T = SS_R + SS_E\]








Component
Name
Meaning




\(SS_T\)
Total Sum of Squares
Total variation in Y


\(SS_R\)
Regression Sum of Squares
Variation explained by the model


\(SS_E\)
Error Sum of Squares
Variation unexplained (due to random error)



The coefficient of determination \(R^2\) is:
\[R^2 = \frac{SS_R}{SS_T} = r^2\]
It represents the percentage of variation in Y explained by X.
Plant data example: \(r = 0.99\) → \(R^2 = 0.99^2 \approx 0.98\) → 98% of the variation in Height is explained by Age.
If \(R^2\) is very small (e.g. 17%), it means the model explains very little variation — there are likely other important variables missing from the model.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
2.11 ANOVA Table for Regression
Just like in ANOVA, we build a table to test the significance of the regression:



Source
df
SS
MS
F




Regression
1
\(SS_R\)
\(MS_R = SS_R / 1\)
\(F = MS_R / MS_E\)


Residuals
\(n-2\)
\(SS_E\)
\(MS_E = SS_E/(n-2)\)



Total
\(n-1\)
\(SS_T\)





The null hypothesis being tested is:
\[H_0: \beta_1 = 0 \quad \text{(no linear relationship)}\] \[H_1: \beta_1 \neq 0 \quad \text{(a linear relationship exists)}\]
If the F-statistic exceeds the critical value (or if p-value < 0.05), we reject \(H_0\) and conclude the slope is significantly different from zero.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
4.1 Example 1 — Plant Growth Data
Variables: Age (weeks) = X, Height (cm) = Y
Data: 10 observations
Age    <- c(2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 8)
Height <- c(4, 5, 9, 11, 12, 14, 17, 21, 22, 24)
plant  <- tibble(Age, Height)

# Scatter plot and correlation
plot(Height ~ Age, data = plant)
cor(plant$Height, plant$Age)

# Fit model
plant_model <- lm(Height ~ Age, data = plant)
summary(plant_model)
summary.aov(plant_model)

# Plot regression line
plot(Height ~ Age)
abline(plant_model)

# Diagnostic plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(plant_model)
qqPlot(plant_model)  # from library(car)
Fitted model result: \[\hat{y} = -1.0299 + 3.0469 \, x\]
Interpretation of slope: For every additional week of age, height increases by 3.047 cm.
R² = 0.9875 → 98.75% of variation in Height is explained by Age.
p-value < 0.001 → Age is a statistically significant predictor of Height.

title: Week 8 – Simple Linear Regression
text:
4.2 Example 2 — Decay Data (Transformation Required)
Variables: Time = X, Amount of organic material = Y
Dataset: Decay.txt
This dataset does not satisfy the linearity assumption — the relationship between amount and time is non-linear (exponential decay). A log transformation is applied to linearise it.
Decay <- read_tsv("Decay.txt")

# Check distribution and relationship
hist(Decay$amount)
plot(Decay$amount ~ Decay$time)

# Fit linear model to original data (model 1 — likely a poor fit)
model1 <- lm(amount ~ time, data = Decay)
summary(model1)
plot(model1)

# Try log transformation
plot(log(amount) ~ time, data = Decay)  # should look more linear

# Fit linear model to log-transformed data (model 2)
model2 <- lm(log(amount) ~ time, data = Decay)
summary(model2)

# Back-transform the equation:
# log(amount) = 4.547386 - 0.068528 * time
# => amount = exp(4.547386) * exp(-0.068528 * time)
# => amount = 94.385 * exp(-0.068528 * time)

# Prediction at time = 15:
# amount = 94.385 * exp(-0.068528 * 15) ≈ 33.77

# Optional: fit non-linear model directly (model 3)
model3 <- nls(amount ~ a * exp(b * time), data = Decay,
              start = list(a = 1, b = -0.1))
summary(model3)
Key concept: When the raw data violates linearity, try a log (or other) transformation. After transforming and fitting, back-transform the equation to interpret it in terms of the original variable.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
3.1 Why Communication Matters
One of the most important skills for a data scientist is the ability to communicate results clearly to people who are not data scientists. The best model in the world has no value if nobody understands it or acts on it.
Key guidelines:

Focus on business value, not the machine learning technique. Nobody in business wants to hear about logistic regression – they want to know how many customers you will keep or how much money will be saved.
Know your audience. A process engineer understands box plots and statistics. A marketing analyst may not.
Many data science projects do not need machine learning. Creating good dashboards and visualisations is often more valuable.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
section: 3.2 Three Ways to Describe the Same Model
text:
3.2 Three Ways to Describe the Same Model
Here is an example of three ways to explain the same churn prediction model:








Summary
Audience
Quality




“Logistic regression with regularisation, recall 80%, precision 66%”
Data scientist peers
Too technical for business


“Identifies 80% of customers who will not return in 30 days; wrong 33% of the time”
Marketing team
Much better – explains the outcome


“Campaign will break even at 10% coupon redemption; at 20% redemption, incremental spend = €11,648, 1,500 extra customers retained”
Business decision makers
Best – connects model to money



Key idea: The third summary uses a mathematical model in Excel (not a machine learning model) to combine the data scientist’s metrics (recall, precision) with the marketing team’s assumptions (coupon cost, redemption rate). This allows both sides to be held accountable for their assumptions.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
4.1 What Is Clustering?
Clustering (also called segmentation) is an unsupervised machine learning task. This means there is no target variable – we are not trying to predict anything. Instead, we are trying to find groups of data that are similar to each other.
Practical example: A supermarket might cluster its customers into groups like “young families”, “students”, and “pensioners”, each of which has different shopping habits. This allows the business to target each group differently.

⚠️ Important: Clusters will almost never be the same size. Expect one large cluster (the “average” customer) and several smaller, more interesting ones.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
section: 4.2 Why We Need to Standardise Data
text:
4.2 Why We Need to Standardise Data
Before clustering, we must standardise all numeric columns so they are on the same scale (0 to 1). Without this, columns with larger numbers (like square metres) will dominate over columns with smaller numbers (like number of bedrooms), even if the smaller column is more meaningful.

4.2.1 Standardisation Formula
\[x_{standardised} = \frac{x - \min(x)}{\max(x) - \min(x)}\]
This converts any numeric value to a number between 0 and 1.
In R:
standardise <- function(x) {
  (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
}

iris$Sepal.Length <- standardise(iris$Sepal.Length)
iris$Sepal.Width  <- standardise(iris$Sepal.Width)
iris$Petal.Length <- standardise(iris$Petal.Length)
iris$Petal.Width  <- standardise(iris$Petal.Width)
After standardisation, the minimum of every column will be 0 and the maximum will be 1.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
4.3 K-Means Clustering

4.3.1 How It Works

You choose k (the number of clusters) before running the algorithm.
The algorithm randomly places k centre points (called centroids).
Each data point is assigned to the nearest centroid.
Each centroid is moved to the average position of all points assigned to it.
Steps 3 and 4 repeat until the assignments stop changing.

The algorithm is not guaranteed to find the perfect solution, so it is usually run multiple times (e.g. nstart = 10) and the best result is kept.
K-means tries to minimise the within-cluster variation – it wants the points inside each cluster to be as close together as possible.


4.3.2 R Code: K-Means on the Iris Dataset
# Run k-means with 3 clusters, 10 random starts
k_clusters <- kmeans(iris[, 1:4], centers = 3, nstart = 10)

# View results
k_clusters
The output shows: - The cluster means (centre of each cluster for each variable) - Which cluster each data point belongs to - The within-cluster sum of squares (lower = tighter clusters)


4.3.3 How to Choose k: The Elbow Method
If you do not know the number of clusters in advance, use the elbow method:

Run k-means for k = 1, 2, 3, …, 10.
Plot the total within-cluster sum of squares for each k.
Find the point where the line “bends” like an elbow – that is your best k.

tot.withinss <- c()

for (k in 1:10) {
  k_clusters <- kmeans(iris[, 1:4], k, nstart = 10)
  tot.withinss <- c(tot.withinss, k_clusters$tot.withinss)
}

plot(main = "Selecting k: Elbow Method", tot.withinss, type = "l")

The curve drops steeply at first, then flattens out. The “elbow” (where the flattening begins) indicates the best number of clusters. For the iris dataset, the elbow is at k = 3.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
4.4 Hierarchical Clustering

4.4.1 How It Works
Hierarchical clustering is a bottom-up approach:

Every data point starts as its own cluster (150 points = 150 clusters).
The two closest clusters are merged into one.
Step 2 repeats until all points are in a single cluster.

The result is shown as a dendrogram (a tree diagram). You can then “cut” the tree at any height to get the number of clusters you want.
Advantage over k-means: You do not need to choose k in advance.


4.4.2 Reading a Dendrogram

Draw a horizontal line across the tree at any height.
Count how many vertical lines it crosses – that is the number of clusters.
The vertical distance between merges tells you how different the clusters are. Larger gaps = more distinct clusters.



4.4.3 R Code: Hierarchical Clustering on the Iris Dataset
# Calculate distances between points, then cluster
h_clusters <- hclust(dist(iris[, 1:4]))

# Cut the tree to get 3 clusters
clusterId <- cutree(h_clusters, 3)

# Add the cluster ID back to the data
iris$clusterId <- as.factor(clusterId)

# Plot the dendrogram
plot(h_clusters)

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
4.5 Interpreting Clusters
After clustering, a common question is: what do the clusters mean?
The answer is to use the same exploratory data analysis techniques from earlier weeks – but instead of colouring plots by a target variable, colour them by cluster ID.
library(ggplot2)

# Scatter plots coloured by cluster
p1 <- qplot(Sepal.Width, Sepal.Length, data = iris,
            colour = clusterId, main = "Sepal.Width vs Sepal.Length")

p2 <- qplot(Petal.Length, Petal.Width, data = iris,
            colour = clusterId, main = "Petal.Length vs Petal.Width")

gridExtra::grid.arrange(p1, p2)
Look for patterns in the plots: - Do certain clusters have higher values on certain variables? - Can you give a meaningful name to each cluster? (e.g. “Large flowers”, “Small flowers”)

4.5.1 Comparing Clusters to a Known Variable
Even though clustering does not use the Species column, we can check how well the clusters match it afterwards:
table(iris$clusterId, iris$Species)
A good match between clusters and the known species confirms the clustering is working well.

🔑 Key reminder: Clustering does not aim to create equal-sized groups. In real retail data, you might find 80% of customers in one large “average” cluster, and small but very interesting clusters of students, pensioners, or high-spending customers.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
5.1 What Are They?
Recommendation engines are systems that suggest products to customers based on what other customers bought. Examples:

“People who bought this also bought…”
“These items are commonly purchased together”

The most common algorithm for this is the Apriori algorithm, which finds patterns of items bought together in transactions (called market basket analysis).

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
5.2 Three Key Metrics

5.2.1 1. Support
How common is this item or item set?
\[\text{Support}(X) = \frac{\text{Number of transactions containing } X}{\text{Total number of transactions}}\]
Support is used to filter out rare items. If an item only appears in 0.01% of transactions, any rule involving it will not be useful in practice.


5.2.2 2. Confidence
If a customer buys X, how likely are they to also buy Y?
\[\text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Support}(X \text{ and } Y)}{\text{Support}(X)}\]
This measures the strength of the rule “if X then Y”.


5.2.3 3. Lift
Is the relationship between X and Y stronger than what we would expect by chance?
\[\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Support}(X \text{ and } Y)}{\text{Support}(X) \times \text{Support}(Y)}\]



Lift value
Meaning




> 1
Buying X makes buying Y more likely than normal


= 1
X and Y are bought independently of each other


< 1
Buying X makes buying Y less likely than normal




🔑 Lift is the most important metric. Select rules with the highest lift. A lift of 3.0 means a customer is 3× more likely to buy Y when they have X in their basket.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
section: 5.3 R Code: Apriori on the Groceries Dataset
text:
5.3 R Code: Apriori on the Groceries Dataset
library(arules)
library(arulesViz)

# Load the built-in grocery dataset
data("Groceries")
summary(Groceries)

# Look at the first 3 transactions
inspect(head(Groceries, 3))

# See the most frequently bought items
freq <- itemFrequency(Groceries, type = "absolute")
freq <- sort(freq, decreasing = TRUE)
head(freq)

# Plot item frequencies for top 20 items
itemFrequencyPlot(Groceries, topN = 20)

5.3.1 Running the Apriori Algorithm
# Items must appear in at least 1% of transactions
# Rules must have at least 30% confidence
support    <- 0.01
confidence <- 0.30
parameters <- list(support = support, confidence = confidence, target = "rules")

rules <- apriori(Groceries, parameter = parameters)

# View rules as a data frame
dfRules <- data.frame(item_set = labels(rules), quality = rules@quality)
head(dfRules)


5.3.2 Plotting the Rules
# Scatter plot of all rules
plot(rules)

# Network graph of only the strongest rules (lift >= 3)
rulesLift <- subset(rules, lift >= 3.0)
plot(rulesLift, method = "graph")

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
5.4 Verifying Calculations Manually
You can verify what the algorithm is doing by calculating the metrics by hand:
# Convert sparse matrix to full data frame
dfTrans <- data.frame(as(Groceries, "matrix"))

# Calculate support, confidence, and lift for beef => root vegetables
support    <- sum(dfTrans$beef & dfTrans$root.vegetables) / nrow(dfTrans)
confidence <- sum(dfTrans$beef & dfTrans$root.vegetables) / sum(dfTrans$beef)
lift       <- support / ((sum(dfTrans$beef) / nrow(dfTrans)) *
                         (sum(dfTrans$root.vegetables) / nrow(dfTrans)))

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
5.5 Applying Recommendation Rules in Practice
Things to consider when using recommendations in a real business:

Transactional vs. member-based: You can group items by transaction ID (what is in one basket right now) or by member ID (what a person has bought over their whole history). Member-based rules can capture purchases that happen months apart (e.g. a cyclist buys a bike, then later a helmet, then cycling shoes).
Use segmentation + recommendations together: Run the recommendation engine separately on each customer segment. This gives much more targeted and relevant suggestions.
Substitute vs. complement products:

A substitute is a similar product (e.g. a different brand of TV). Goal: upsell (buy a better version).
A complement is a product bought alongside the main product (e.g. a TV cable). Goal: cross-sell (buy something extra).

Avoid bombarding customers. If someone just bought a TV, showing them more TV ads for 6 months is annoying and ineffective.
Ethics matter. A famous example: Target (US supermarket) identified pregnant women from their shopping habits and sent them baby product offers. One teenager’s father received the offers before he knew about the pregnancy. Always consider how customers will feel about personalised targeting.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
6.1 What Is a Hyperparameter?
In statistics, a parameter usually means a variable in your data (e.g. age, income).
In machine learning, a hyperparameter is a setting of the algorithm itself – something you choose before training, not learned from the data.
Examples:







Algorithm
Hyperparameter examples




Decision Tree
Splitting criterion (Gini, entropy)


Support Vector Machine
Kernel function (linear, RBF)


Neural Network
Number of layers, nodes per layer, learning rate


Random Forest
Number of trees (ntree), features per tree (mtry)



The process of finding the best set of hyperparameters is called hyperparameter optimisation.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
6.2 Methods for Hyperparameter Optimisation

6.2.1 Grid Search
Try every possible combination of hyperparameters. If you have 4 hyperparameters with 2, 5, 3, and 2 possible values each:
\[2 \times 5 \times 3 \times 2 = 60 \text{ experiments}\]
Grid search is exhaustive but very slow for large numbers of hyperparameters.


6.2.2 Random Search
Select combinations at random. Useful when you cannot afford to run all combinations. Less thorough than grid search, but much faster.


6.2.3 Bayesian Search
Starts like random search, then focuses on the most promising area based on the results seen so far. Think of it like the hot/cold game – it “gets warmer” and homes in on the best region.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
section: 6.3 R Code: Manual Hyperparameter Search (Random Forest)
text:
6.3 R Code: Manual Hyperparameter Search (Random Forest)
library(randomForest)

# Define hyperparameter values to try
trees <- c(50, 200, 500)
mtrys <- c(4, 6)

best_accuracy <- 0

for (v1 in trees) {
  for (v2 in mtrys) {
    rf_model <- randomForest(as.factor(Survived) ~ .,
                             data  = train_data,
                             ntree = v1,
                             mtry  = v2)

    pr  <- predict(rf_model, newdata = test_data, type = "class")
    pr  <- factor(pr, levels = c(1, 0))
    tab <- table(pr, actuals)
    accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab)) / sum(tab)

    if (accuracy > best_accuracy) {
      best_tree     <- v1
      best_mtry     <- v2
      best_accuracy <- accuracy
    }
  }
}

print(paste0("Best accuracy = ", round(best_accuracy, 2),
             ", ntree = ", best_tree,
             ", mtry = ", best_mtry))

This runs 6 experiments (3 tree values × 2 mtry values). In practice, use the caret or tidymodels packages to do this automatically and more rigorously.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
6.4 Important Warnings

6.4.1 You Need THREE Data Splits for Hyperparameter Optimisation
When optimising hyperparameters, do not keep evaluating on the same test set – you are effectively peeking at the answers. Use:

Training set – train the model
Validation set – compare hyperparameter combinations
Test set – used only once at the very end to report final accuracy

A typical split might be 70% / 15% / 15%.


6.4.2 Hyperparameter Optimisation Is NOT the First Step

⚠️ Feature engineering and data quality matter much more than hyperparameter tuning. Most algorithms give reasonable results with default settings. Only optimise hyperparameters once your data preparation is solid.

title: STAT9013 – Week 7 Study Guide
text:
6.5 AutoML
Some tools (e.g. h2o.ai, AWS SageMaker) automate the whole process: selecting the algorithm AND tuning the hyperparameters. This is called AutoML.

Useful for extracting the last small improvement in performance.
Can run for days on large datasets using multiple cloud machines.
The improvement is usually small (often less than 1% accuracy difference between models).
Should be the last step in your workflow, not the first.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
3.1 What is it?
The Titanic dataset is one of the most well-known datasets in machine learning. It records information about passengers aboard the RMS Titanic, which sank in April 1912. The central question is:

Can we build a model that predicts which passengers survived the Titanic disaster?

This is a binary classification problem — for each passenger, the model must predict one of two outcomes: survived (1) or did not survive (0).

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
3.2 Variables in the Dataset








Variable
Description
Notes




PassengerId
Unique ID for each passenger
Not useful for prediction


Survived
0 = No, 1 = Yes
Target variable


Pclass
Ticket class: 1 = 1st, 2 = 2nd, 3 = 3rd
Very important predictor


Name
Passenger’s full name
Unique — drop it


Sex
Gender
Very strong predictor


Age
Age in years
Has many missing values


SibSp
# of siblings/spouses aboard
Combined with Parch


Parch
# of parents/children aboard
Combined with SibSp


Ticket
Ticket number
Likely not useful as-is


Fare
Price paid for the ticket
Correlated with Pclass


Cabin
Cabin number
Lots of missing values


Embarked
Port of embarkation: C, Q, or S
Some predictive signal

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
3.3 Loading the Data in R
library(tidyverse)
library(psych)

# Install the titanic package if not available
if (!require("titanic"))
  devtools::install_github("paulhendricks/titanic")
library("titanic")

data(titanic_train)
str(titanic_train)

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
4.1 Why Explore Data Before Modelling?
Data exploration is a critical step before building any machine learning model. It helps you:

Understand what the data looks like
Identify missing values and decide how to handle them
Spot variables that are likely to be useful predictors
Detect relationships between predictor variables (not just between each variable and the target)
Avoid mistakes that would silently damage your model’s quality


Rule of thumb: Always know your data before you model it. A model built on poorly understood data will produce unreliable results.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
section: 4.2 Checking Unique Values and Missing Data
text:
4.2 Checking Unique Values and Missing Data
# Count unique values per column
uniq_len <- function(x) { length(unique(x)) }
sapply(titanic_train, uniq_len)

# Count missing values (NAs or empty strings)
countBlank <- function(x) { sum(is.na(x) | x == "") }
sapply(titanic_train, countBlank)
What to look for:

Columns with as many unique values as rows (e.g. PassengerId, Name) → These are IDs, not features. Drop them.
Columns with too many missing values (e.g. Cabin has 687 missing out of 891) → Consider dropping or imputing.
Unexpected number of unique values (e.g. Embarked shows 4 unique values when only 3 are expected) → Signals there are blank/null entries that need fixing.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
section: 4.3 Key Findings from the Titanic Data
text:
4.3 Key Findings from the Titanic Data

4.3.1 Family Size vs. Survival
A new variable family is created by adding SibSp + Parch to represent total family members travelling together.
titanic_train$family <- titanic_train$SibSp + titanic_train$Parch
titanic_train$Survived <- factor(titanic_train$Survived)

ggplot(titanic_train, aes(x = family, fill = factor(Survived))) +
  geom_bar(stat = 'count', position = 'dodge') +
  labs(x = 'Family Size') +
  ggtitle("Family size v Survived")
Finding: Solo travellers had the lowest survival rate. Passengers in small family groups (1–3 others) had noticeably higher survival rates. Very large families (6+) had very few records, so conclusions there are not reliable.
Interpretation: Being in a family likely means children were present, and children were given priority in lifeboats.


4.3.2 Ticket Class vs. Survival
p01 <- ggplot(titanic_train, aes(Pclass, fill = Survived)) +
  geom_bar(position = "stack") + ggtitle("By counts")

p02 <- ggplot(titanic_train, aes(Pclass, fill = Survived)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) + ggtitle("By proportion")

gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, nrow = 1, top = 'Survived by Class')
Finding:

1st class: ~63% survived
2nd class: ~47% survived
3rd class: ~24% survived

Important tip — plot both counts AND proportions:

Counts tell you how many passengers are in each group (the effect size)
Proportions tell you how different the survival rate is from the baseline of 38% (the signal strength)

Both matter. A strong signal (e.g. 90% survival) in a group with only 10 records is not very useful — it barely affects the overall model.


4.3.3 Gender vs. Survival
Finding:

Female passengers: ~74% survived
Male passengers: ~19% survived

Gender is the single strongest predictor in this dataset. This aligns with the historical “women and children first” policy used when loading lifeboats.


4.3.4 Fixing the Embarked Variable
Two passengers had blank values for Embarked. Since Southampton was by far the most common embarkation port, those blanks were reassigned to S.
titanic_train[titanic_train$Embarked == "", ]$Embarked <- 'S'
Why not create a new “Unknown” category? Because with only 2 records, assigning them a unique value X could cause the model to over-fit on that tiny group. The safer choice is to merge them into the most common category.


4.3.5 Fare vs. Survival
A box plot of Fare by Survived shows that passengers who paid higher fares had a somewhat higher survival rate. However, fare is closely correlated with ticket class — they carry overlapping information.


4.3.6 Age vs. Survival — Creating a Derived Variable
A simple box plot of Age vs. Survived doesn’t show a strong difference. This is because the real signal is not about age in general, but specifically about children.
temp <- titanic_train[!is.na(titanic_train$Age), ]
temp$is_child <- ifelse(temp$Age < 17, 1, 0)
Children (under 17) had roughly a 50% survival rate vs. about 35% for adults. Creating a binary is_child variable helps the machine learning algorithm pick up on this threshold effect.

Key concept — derived variables: Sometimes the raw variable is not as useful as a transformed or binned version of it. Think about what threshold or transformation would best capture the relationship with the target.



4.3.7 Pairs Plot
columns <- c("Pclass", "Age", "Fare", "family")
pairs.panels(titanic_train[, columns], method = "pearson",
             hist.col = "#00AFBB", density = TRUE, ellipses = FALSE)
Finding: The strongest correlation among predictors is between Pclass and Fare (–0.55), which makes sense — first class passengers paid much more.
Why this matters: If two predictor variables are highly correlated, they carry redundant information. The model may just pick one of them and largely ignore the other.


4.3.8 Embarkation and Ticket Class
A deeper investigation reveals why passengers from Cherbourg (C) had a higher survival rate: a much higher proportion of first-class passengers boarded at Cherbourg compared to Queenstown or Southampton. The survival signal from Embarked is largely already captured by Pclass.
This is an example of a variable that appears predictive but is actually a proxy for another variable. Including such variables in a model can be misleading.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
5.1 What is it?
Supervised learning means training a model on data where we already know the correct answers (the labels), so the model learns to predict those labels for new, unseen data.
Classification is a type of supervised learning where the output is a category (e.g. survived / not survived, spam / not spam, cat / dog).

Binary classification: two possible outcomes (like Titanic survived yes/no)
Multi-class classification: three or more outcomes (like Iris species: setosa / versicolor / virginica)

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
5.2 Common Classification Algorithms







Algorithm
Key Characteristic




Decision Trees
Simple, interpretable, easy to visualise


Logistic Regression
Probabilistic, good baseline model


Support Vector Machines
Strong with high-dimensional data


Naive Bayes
Fast, works well with text


Random Forests
Ensemble of trees, very powerful


Gradient Boosted Trees
Often best on tabular data


Deep Learning
Best for images, text, audio

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
6.1 The Core Workflow
library(rpart)
data(iris)

# 1. Set a random seed for reproducibility
set.seed(1987)

# 2. Split data into training (90%) and test (10%)
nobs <- nrow(iris)
train <- sample(nobs, 0.9 * nobs)
test <- setdiff(seq_len(nobs), train)

# 3. Train the model on training data ONLY
rpart_model <- rpart(Species ~ ., data = iris[train, ], method = "class")

# 4. Get predictions on the test set
pr <- predict(rpart_model, newdata = iris[test, ], type = "class")

# 5. Evaluate performance
tab <- table(pr, iris[test, ]$Species, dnn = c("predicted", "actual"))
print(tab)

accuracy <- 100 * sum(diag(tab)) / sum(tab)
print(paste('Model accuracy on test data =', round(accuracy, 2)))

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
section: 6.2 Why Split Into Train and Test?
text:
6.2 Why Split Into Train and Test?
A model will always look better on data it was trained on, because it has already “seen” that data. Evaluating on training data is like giving someone an exam with the answer sheet in front of them — it tells you nothing about how well they actually understand the material.
The test set must be:

Created before building the model
Completely separate from the training set (no overlap)
Representative of data the model will see in the real world


Important: The evaluation metrics you report should come from the test set, not the training set.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
6.3 Reproducible Data Science
set.seed(1987)  # Always set this before any random operations
Setting a random seed ensures that every time the code is run (on any machine), it produces the exact same train/test split. This is essential for reproducible data science — other people must be able to reproduce your results exactly, or your findings cannot be trusted.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
6.4 What is Overfitting / Overtraining?
Overfitting happens when a model learns the training data too well — including its noise and quirks — and fails to generalise to new data.
Example: If the model memorises that the 2 passengers with a blank Embarked value both survived, it will predict survival for any future record with a blank embarkation. This is not a real pattern, just noise — and the model will perform poorly on new data.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
6.5 Model Drift and Data Drift

Data drift: The distribution of real-world data changes over time, so the model (trained on older data) becomes less accurate.
Model drift: The relationship between the input variables and the target variable changes over time.

Both of these mean that machine learning models need to be monitored and periodically retrained.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
7.1 What is a Decision Tree?
A decision tree is one of the simplest and most intuitive machine learning algorithms. It works by splitting the data into groups based on a series of yes/no questions (conditions).
Analogy: Imagine a game of 20 questions. Each question splits the possibilities in two, and you keep narrowing down until you reach a final answer.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
7.2 How it Works

Start with all the training data at the root node
Find the best attribute (column) and the best split point that separates the data into more “pure” groups
Create two branches (left = condition is true, right = condition is false)
Repeat for each sub-group (node) until:

All records in a node belong to the same class (it becomes a leaf node), or
There are no more useful attributes to split on

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
section: 7.3 How to Choose the Best Split?
text:
7.3 How to Choose the Best Split?
The algorithm uses a measure of impurity to decide which split is best. The goal is always to reduce impurity — i.e. to create groups that are as “pure” (dominated by a single class) as possible.
Two common impurity measures:
\[\text{Entropy}(t) = -\sum_{i=0}^{c-1} p(i|t) \log_2 p(i|t)\]
\[\text{Gini}(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{c-1} [p(i|t)]^2\]
Where c is the number of classes and p(i|t) is the probability that a record at node t belongs to class i.
Information Gain is calculated by comparing the impurity of the parent node to the weighted average impurity of the child nodes after the split. The split with the highest information gain is chosen.
Intuition: A node where 50% of records are class 0 and 50% are class 1 is maximally impure (entropy = 1). A node where 100% of records are the same class is perfectly pure (entropy = 0).

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
7.4 Advantages and Disadvantages







Advantages
Disadvantages




Easy to understand and visualise
Other algorithms usually outperform it


Can deal with non-linear data
Tends to overfit (especially deep trees)


No need to normalise data
Sensitive to small changes in data


Can be expressed as if/then rules
Does not handle missing data well

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
7.5 Handling Numeric Data
Since every node is a yes/no condition, numeric variables are handled by splitting on a threshold value (e.g. Age >= 13, Fare >= 23). The algorithm finds the best threshold automatically.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
7.6 Pruning
Decision trees can grow very large and complex, overfitting the training data. Pruning means cutting off some of the lower branches to simplify the tree and improve generalisation.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
7.7 Example — Titanic Decision Tree
The decision tree built on the Titanic dataset finds the following key splits (starting from the root):

Sex = male? → Most important split. Males generally did not survive.
For males: Age >= 13? → Younger boys had better survival odds
For females: Pclass >= 3? → 3rd class females had lower survival rates
Further splits on Fare and SibSp

This mirrors exactly what we expected from the exploratory analysis: gender is the most important predictor, followed by ticket class and age.

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
8.1 What is a Confusion Matrix?
After building a model, we compare its predictions against the actual values on the test set. The results are displayed in a confusion matrix.
For a binary classification problem (survived = 1, not survived = 0):




Actual: 0
Actual: 1




Predicted: 0
True Negative (TN)
False Negative (FN)


Predicted: 1
False Positive (FP)
True Positive (TP)

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
8.2 Titanic Example
          Actual
Predicted  0   1
        0  50  19   ← Predicted "not survived"
        1   4  17   ← Predicted "survived"

TN = 50: Correctly predicted “did not survive”
FN = 19: Person survived, but model predicted they didn’t (missed survivors)
FP = 4: Person didn’t survive, but model predicted they did
TP = 17: Correctly predicted “survived”

title: Data Mining – Class 3 Notes
text:
8.3 Accuracy
\[\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{17 + 50}{90} \approx 74\%\]

Note to self: Accuracy is not always the best metric — it can be misleading when classes are imbalanced. For example, if only 5% of passengers survived, a model that always predicts “not survived” would be 95% accurate but completely useless. More metrics (precision, recall, F1) will be covered in future classes.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
text: Week 1 covered the foundations: the differences between data, information, and knowledge; definitions of AI, Machine Learning, and Deep Learning; the CRISP-DM and SEMMA workflows; types of ML (supervised, unsupervised, reinforcement learning); and a worked linear regression example.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
text: Week 1 covered the foundations: the differences between data, information, and knowledge; definitions of AI, Machine Learning, and Deep Learning; the CRISP-DM and SEMMA workflows; types of ML (supervised, unsupervised, reinforcement learning); and a worked linear regression example.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
section: 2 CRISP-DM and the Importance of Business Understanding
text:
2 CRISP-DM and the Importance of Business Understanding
Before diving into statistics and code, the lecture emphasised a critical lesson: the data understanding and data preparation stages of CRISP-DM are often more important than the modelling stage itself. The CRISP-DM cycle has six phases — Business Understanding, Data Understanding, Data Preparation, Modelling, Evaluation, and Deployment — and notice that two of those six are entirely about working with the data before any model is built.

2.1 The PayPal Churn Case Study
This real-world example illustrated how business understanding can matter more than fancy algorithms. PayPal was losing around 1 million merchants per year. After 17 years of trying various approaches, the problem was finally tackled not with advanced ML but with careful problem definition using simple tools (spreadsheets and SQL).
Key steps in their approach:

Exclude accounts that already cancelled: By the time someone cancels, it is too late — they have already migrated to a competitor like Stripe. Nothing can be done to win them back.
Exclude “one-and-dones”: Merchants who signed up briefly, had little revenue, and disappeared. Not worth chasing because the revenue was negligible.
Separate activation from retention: New merchants who had problems getting started (failed payments, onboarding issues) are a different problem for a different team. This is not churn — it is an onboarding issue.
Exclude false positives: Seasonal businesses (Halloween costume stores), subscription processors who only transact once a month, and merchants who were kicked off for violating terms.
Focus on revenue churn, not account churn: 90% of revenue came from 10% of merchants. Losing a merchant doing €100,000/month matters far more than losing one doing €500/month.

After all these exclusions, they looked at individual cases for their highest-value remaining merchants, identified 20 “killer scenarios” causing churn, and fixed them one by one — often just by having customer service call the merchant.
The takeaway: Always ask “What am I actually trying to solve?” before reaching for a machine learning model. Sometimes the best solution is careful problem decomposition combined with domain expertise.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
section: 3 Basic Statistics: Means, Medians, Quartiles, and IQR
text:
3 Basic Statistics: Means, Medians, Quartiles, and IQR

3.1 Mean vs Median

Mean: The arithmetic average — sum all values and divide by the count. Sensitive to extreme values (outliers).
Median: The middle value when data is sorted from smallest to largest. Robust to outliers.

Why the difference matters: Imagine 11 people in a room, each earning around €100,000. The mean salary is roughly €100,000 and the median is also about €100,000 — they agree. Now Bill Gates and Jeff Bezos walk in. The mean salary jumps to perhaps €30 million, but the median barely changes — it just moves from the 6th to the 7th person’s salary, still around €100,000.
The mean no longer represents anyone in the group. The median still tells you what the “typical” person earns. This is why the media report the median age of COVID deaths rather than the average — it gives a more meaningful picture of who is affected.
Rule of thumb: When data is skewed or contains outliers, the median is usually more informative than the mean.


3.2 Quartiles and Interquartile Range (IQR)
To calculate quartiles:

Order the data from smallest to largest.
Split it into four equal groups.
Each group is a “quartile.”


Q1 (25th percentile): The boundary between the lowest 25% and the rest.
Q2 (50th percentile): This is the median.
Q3 (75th percentile): The boundary between the top 25% and the rest.
IQR = Q3 − Q1: The range covering the middle 50% of the data.

The IQR is useful because it tells you how spread out the “core” of your data is, ignoring extremes.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
text:
4 Visualising Distributions: Univariate Plots
Univariate plots examine a single variable at a time. The three main types covered are histograms, density plots, and box plots. They all show the distribution (the spread and shape) of your data, but in different ways.

4.1 Histograms
A histogram divides the data into “bins” (ranges) and counts how many values fall into each bin. It shows frequency on the y-axis and the variable’s values on the x-axis. Think of it as a bar chart of your data’s distribution.


Code
qplot(data, geom = "histogram", bins = 100)




4.2 Density Plots
A density plot is a smoothed version of a histogram. Instead of bars, you get a continuous curve. The y-axis shows density (not raw count), and the area under the curve sums to 1. It is useful for seeing the overall shape of the distribution without being distracted by bin choices.


Code
qplot(data, geom = "density")




4.3 Box Plots
A box plot (or box-and-whisker plot) packs a lot of information into a compact display:

The box spans from Q1 to Q3 (the IQR — the middle 50% of data).
The line inside the box is the median.
The whiskers extend to the most extreme data points that are within 1.5 × IQR from the edges of the box.
Points beyond the whiskers are considered outliers.

Box plots are extremely useful for statisticians and data scientists, but as the lecturer noted, they are often not well understood by non-technical business users. So use them for your own analysis, but consider simpler visuals when presenting to stakeholders.


Code
qplot(data, geom = "boxplot") + coord_flip()




4.4 The drawPlots Helper Function
The lecture introduced a handy helper function that draws all three plots side by side, with the mean shown in blue and the median in green:


Code
drawPlots <- function(data, title) {
  p01 <- qplot(data, geom = "histogram", bins = 100) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = mean(data), size = 2, color = "blue") +
    geom_vline(xintercept = median(data), size = 2, color = "green")
  p02 <- qplot(data, geom = "density") +
    geom_vline(xintercept = mean(data), size = 2, color = "blue") +
    geom_vline(xintercept = median(data), size = 2, color = "green")
  p03 <- qplot(data, geom = "boxplot") +
    coord_flip() +
    geom_point(aes(x = mean(data), y = 0), size = 3, color = "blue")
  gridExtra::grid.arrange(p01, p02, p03, nrow = 1, top = title)
}

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
section: 5 Skewness: What It Is and Why It Matters
text:
5 Skewness: What It Is and Why It Matters

5.1 What Is Skewness?
Skewness describes how asymmetric a distribution is compared to a normal (bell-shaped) distribution.

Normal distribution: Symmetric. The mean and median are approximately equal. The histogram/density plot looks like a bell curve, centred around the middle.
Right (positive) skew: The “tail” of the distribution stretches to the right. Most values are clustered on the left, but a few very large values pull the mean to the right. Mean > Median.
Left (negative) skew: The tail stretches to the left. Most values cluster on the right, but a few very small values pull the mean left. Mean < Median.



Code
# Normal distribution — mean ≈ median
data <- rnorm(10000, mean = 0, sd = 1)
drawPlots(data, "Normal distribution (median ~= mean)")

# Left (negative) skew — median > mean
data <- rbeta(10000, 5, 1)
drawPlots(data, "Left skewed (median-green > mean-blue)")

# Right (positive) skew — median < mean
data <- rbeta(10000, 1, 5)
drawPlots(data, "Right skewed (median-green < mean-blue)")




5.2 Real-World Examples of Skewed Data
A lot of real-life data is skewed:

Income: Most people earn moderate amounts, but a few earn enormously high salaries (right skew).
Average spend per customer: Most customers spend a little, a few spend a lot.
Distance travelled to work: Most people live reasonably close; some commute very long distances.
Animal body weights: Most mammals are small; a few (elephants, whales) are extremely large.



5.3 Why Skewness Matters for Machine Learning
The lecture demonstrated this using the mammals dataset (from the openintro package), which contains body weight and brain weight for various mammals.
The problem: Both body weight and brain weight are extremely right-skewed — most mammals are small, but a few (like elephants) are huge. When you plot the raw data, almost all points cluster near zero, and you can barely see any relationship.
Step 1 — Build a model on raw data:


Code
model1 <- lm(body_wt ~ brain_wt, data = mammals)
r_sq1 <- cor(model1$fitted.values, mammals$body_wt)^2
# R² ≈ 0.87


Step 2 — Apply a log transformation:
Taking the logarithm of skewed data compresses the large values and stretches the small ones, making the distribution more symmetrical (closer to normal).


Code
mammals$log_body_wt <- log(mammals$body_wt)
mammals$log_brain_wt <- log(mammals$brain_wt)


When you plot the log-transformed data, you can now clearly see a linear relationship between log(brain weight) and log(body weight).
Step 3 — Build a model on transformed data:


Code
model2 <- lm(log_body_wt ~ log_brain_wt, data = mammals)
r_sq2 <- cor(model2$fitted.values, mammals$log_body_wt)^2
# R² ≈ 0.92


The R² improved from 0.87 to 0.92. As the slides noted, this represents a 37.79% reduction in error rate. That is a significant improvement gained purely from transforming the data — not from changing the algorithm.
Key insight: If your data is skewed, consider applying a log transformation (or other transformations) before modelling. This is a form of feature engineering and can dramatically improve model performance.

title: STAT9013 - Data Mining: Week 2
text:
6 Packages and the qplot Function

6.1 Required Packages


Code
library(ggplot2)     # Plotting — qplot and ggplot functions
library(gridExtra)   # Arrange multiple plots in a grid
library(datasets)    # Built-in R datasets (iris, etc.)
